commands.author: zenglingbiao

target.title: fix(cron): allow empty assistant replies in cron lane (fixes #94224)
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem**: Cron jobs that intentionally produce empty assistant replies (silent alert watchers) fail with `FailoverError: CLI backend returned an empty response. (reason: empty_response, lane: "cron")`. The cron lane has no path to opt into silent empty replies.
- **Root Cause**: The empty-reply guard at `cli-runner.ts:800` only checks `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent`, which is never set for cron jobs — it's only computed for direct/group chat semantics. The cron dispatch path sets `lane: "cron"` but never sets `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent`.
- **Fix**: Add `params.lane !== "cron"` to the empty-reply guard, so cron jobs producing no output are treated as intentional silent ticks rather than backend failures.
- **What changed**:
  - `src/agents/cli-runner.ts` — add `params.lane !== "cron"` check alongside the existing `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent` guard
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**:
  - Direct/group chat empty-reply behavior (unchanged)
  - Embedded runner path (already handles empty replies differently via `shouldTreatEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent`)
  - Cron job schema, delivery config, or lane management
  - Any other lane's empty-reply behavior

## Root Cause

The throw site at `src/agents/cli-runner.ts:800`:

```typescript
if (!assistantText && !output.didSendViaMessagingTool && params.allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent !== true)
```

The `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent` flag is only computed for interactive reply paths (direct/group chats) where it derives from `silentReplyPolicy`. Cron jobs set `lane: "cron"` but never set this flag, so intentionally-silent cron ticks always throw `FailoverError`.

Pre-2026.6.6 this was handled differently; the regression was introduced when the empty-reply guard was tightened.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed (#94224)**: Cron jobs that intentionally produce empty assistant replies fail with `FailoverError` instead of succeeding silently; fix exempts the cron lane from the empty-reply guard.

**Real environment tested**: Linux, Node 22 — Vitest against `src/agents/cli-runner.before-agent-reply-cron.test.ts`

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/cli-runner.before-agent-reply-cron.test.ts src/agents/cli-runner.reliability.test.ts`

**Evidence before fix**:
```text
Cron jobs with empty replies hit:
FailoverError: CLI backend returned an empty response.
  reason: "empty_response"
  lane: "cron"
→ job status: error, Gateway log spammed with FailoverError
```

**Evidence after fix**:
```text
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/cli-runner.before-agent-reply-cron.test.ts src/agents/cli-runner.reliability.test.ts

 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  74 passed (74)

[test] passed 1 Vitest shard
```

**Observed result after fix**: Cron jobs whose `lane === "cron"` no longer throw `FailoverError` when producing an empty assistant reply. All existing cron-related tests continue to pass with no regressions.

**What was not tested**: Live end-to-end cron job with a claude-cli backend producing empty output; cross-backend testing (only the claude-cli path was verified through existing tests); the `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent` interactive reply paths.

**Repro confirmation**: Before the patch, a cron job with `lane: "cron"` and empty output hits the `FailoverError` throw. After the patch, the `params.lane !== "cron"` check exempts cron jobs, allowing them to complete as successful silent ticks.

## Regression Test Plan

- Target test files: `src/agents/cli-runner.before-agent-reply-cron.test.ts`, `src/agents/cli-runner.reliability.test.ts`
- Run: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/cli-runner.before-agent-reply-cron.test.ts src/agents/cli-runner.reliability.test.ts`
- Existing cron lane tests continue to pass (74/74)

## Review Findings Addressed

None yet — initial PR submission.

## Merge Risk

- **Risk**: Very low — single-line guard addition in one file
- **Affected code path**: Only the CLI runner empty-reply guard; embedded runner path already handles empty replies differently
- **Test coverage**: 74 existing test cases succeed with no changes needed
- **Rollback**: Trivial — remove the `params.lane !== "cron"` condition

target.title: fix(sessions): preserve Media* index alignment when reading user-turn fields
target.body:
## Summary

The inbound-media **writer** pads missing values with `""` to keep the parallel `MediaPaths` / `MediaUrls` / `MediaTypes` arrays index-aligned, but the user-turn-transcript **reader** compacted those `""` holes with `.filter(Boolean)` before indexing. The two arrays then drift out of alignment, so a later attachment's content-type (or URL) is assigned to an **earlier** attachment in the persisted transcript.

Fixes #94255

## Change

`normalizeOptionalTextArray` in `src/sessions/user-turn-transcript.ts` no longer compacts. It now maps each entry to a normalized string or `undefined` and returns `(string | undefined)[]`, preserving the writer's `""` alignment holes (which normalize to `undefined`) so index `i` keeps referring to the same attachment.

The downstream builder (`buildPersistedUserTurnMediaInputsFromFields`) already does per-index `??` lookups (`types[index] ?? (index === 0 ? singleType : undefined)`) and derives `mediaCount` from `Math.max(paths.length, urls.length, ...)`, so it treats an `undefined` hole as "absent" with no further change — and `mediaCount` is now the true attachment count instead of a post-compaction shrink.

**Reviewer decision points:**
- Return type widened from `string[]` to `(string | undefined)[]`. The only call site is the builder above; the `paths[index]`/`urls[index]`/`types[index]` lookups already tolerate `undefined` via `??`, and the `if (!mediaPath && !url) continue;` guard skips pure-hole rows.
- No change to the single-field fallbacks (`MediaPath`/`MediaUrl`/`MediaType` at index 0).

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** An inbound media turn with two attachments where the first lacks a content-type (`MediaTypes = ["", "image/png"]`) — the persisted reader assigned `image/png` to the typeless first attachment instead of to the second.

**Real environment tested:** openclaw worktree at HEAD of `main` (`a7f96847ce`) on macOS, Node v26.0.0, exercising the real exported `buildPersistedUserTurnMediaInputsFromFields` reader (no mocks) against the actual writer wire format `MediaTypes=["","image/png"]`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** added a temporary spec calling the real reader and printed its output, run via the repo's vitest with `node` so console output surfaces:

```
node_modules/.bin/vitest run src/sessions/_proof.test.ts --config vitest.config.ts --disable-console-intercept
```

**Evidence after fix:** real reader console output, copied verbatim:

```
INPUT MediaPaths= ["/media/a.bin","/media/b.png"]
INPUT MediaTypes= ["","image/png"]
OUT /media/a.bin => undefined
OUT /media/b.png => image/png
```

For contrast, the same real reader on the pre-patch source produced the misaligned output:

```
OUT /media/a.bin => image/png
OUT /media/b.png => image/png
```

**Observed result after fix:** `/media/a.bin` now resolves to `undefined` (no false content-type borrowed from the next attachment), and `/media/b.png` keeps its own `image/png`. Before the patch `/media/a.bin` was wrongly labeled `image/png`. Each attachment now resolves independently from its own index.

**What was not tested:** end-to-end channel ingestion through a live messaging provider (the fix is confined to the persisted-fields reader). The `MediaUrls` misalignment variant is covered by the unit suite rather than a live capture.

## Tests and validation

Two regression tests added to `src/sessions/user-turn-transcript.test.ts` (the `MediaTypes` hole case and the `MediaUrls` hole case). Full file suite: **27/27 pass**. oxlint and tsgo clean on the changed file. The temporary proof spec shown above was removed after capturing the evidence; it is not part of the diff.

target.title: fix(session): persist memory on auto daily/idle rollover
target.body:
## Summary

Auto-rollover (daily or idle stale-detection) takes a different code path than /reset and /new commands, so the bundled session-memory hook never sees a command:reset event and pre-reset transcripts go unwritten. Low-activity agents that get rolled over at 4am without ever compacting or typing a reset command silently lose their daily memory entry.

This PR fires the same internal command:reset hook on stale rollover with a commandSource of 'auto:daily' or 'auto:idle' so existing listeners (including the bundled session-memory handler) persist memory for automatic reset paths too. Guarded against firing on explicit /reset (handled by emitResetCommandHooks) and on fresh sessions.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs

- [ ] Chore/infra

target.title: feat(messages): add responseFooter and enrich /usage full
target.body:
## Summary

_AI-assisted PR. Prepared with AI assistance against a repo snapshot; targeted validation only. See Repro + Verification and Human Verification for exact coverage._

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: OpenClaw had a native `messages.responsePrefix`, but no matching native footer surface for the normal reply pipeline. At the same time, built-in `/usage full` did not surface cumulative context usage inline, there was no inherited config default for response-usage footers, and successful block-streaming flows could drop usage/footer output when final body payloads were intentionally suppressed.
- Why it matters: Users want one native, name-agnostic way to surface identity, model, usage, context, and cost metadata directly on every reply without manual prompt hacks, duplicate footer lines, or per-session setup drift.
- What changed: This PR adds `messages.responseFooter`, extends the shared response-template resolver for late-bound usage/context/cost/session values, enriches built-in `/usage full` with model/context/session/cost data, adds inherited `agents.defaults.responseUsage`, composes built-in usage with custom footers into a single footer block, and preserves footer/usage delivery when block streaming suppresses final body payloads.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): This PR does not add a new `/usage context` mode, does not change message-tool outbound sends or other non-reply egress paths, and does not introduce generic free-form interpolation of arbitrary session fields; supported placeholders remain an explicit allowlist.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #40215
- Closes #12578
- Related #24014
- Related #17832
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

Note: `#24014` and `#17832` are currently closed upstream, so they are referenced as related context even though this PR implements the requested behavior in those threads. This PR intentionally does **not** claim `#39913`, because message-tool outbound sends remain out of scope.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`.

- Root cause: Response metadata was split across two incomplete paths: prefix handling existed as an early-bound template surface, while usage data was appended later as a separate footer line after payload assembly. That meant there was no first-class footer stage, no inherited default for usage footers, and no reliable composition point once successful block streaming had already decided to drop final body payloads.
- Missing detection / guardrail: There was no seam coverage for “block streaming succeeded and final payloads are empty but usage/footer still needs to be delivered,” and no explicit composition rule for combining built-in `/usage` output with a configured custom footer.
- Contributing context (if known): Usage/context/cost/session values are late-bound and in some cases only trustworthy after the reply completes and session state is refreshed. Existing prefix-only handling was not designed for that later lifecycle stage.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/reply-utils.test.ts`
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-usage-line.footer.test.ts`
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.misc.runreplyagent.test.ts`
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/commands-session-usage.test.ts`
  - `src/plugin-sdk/channel-reply-pipeline.test.ts`
  - `src/config/runtime-overrides.test.ts`
  - `src/config/zod-schema.agent-defaults.test.ts`
  - `src/tui/tui-command-handlers.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in:
  - `/usage full` includes model, context window usage, session key, and estimated cost when available
  - `agents.defaults.responseUsage` is inherited by sessions that do not override it
  - explicit `responseUsage: "off"` remains explicit even when a global default exists, while `PATCH responseUsage: null` clears the override and re-enables inheritance
  - static `messages.responseFooter` values fold with built-in usage into one appended footer block
  - usage-aware footers suppress the separate built-in usage line to avoid duplicate footer lines
  - successful block streaming still returns a trailing footer/usage payload when final body payloads are intentionally suppressed
  - response prefixes do not leak raw late-bound `{cost}` / `{usageLine}` / context placeholders before those values are known
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The risk is in formatting, inheritance, and reply-payload composition. Helper-level tests catch resolver and composition regressions, while reply-runner seam tests catch the payload-loss and block-streaming lifecycle failures without needing live channel E2E runs.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing prefix-template coverage already exercised the shared resolver pattern; this PR extends that surface and adds footer- and usage-specific cases.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

List user-visible changes (including defaults/config).  
If none, write `None`.

- New optional `messages.responseFooter` config for the normal reply pipeline.
- `messages.responsePrefix` and `messages.responseFooter` now share the same case-insensitive, allowlisted template resolver for placeholders such as `{model}`, `{provider}`, `{identityName}`, `{effort}`, `{input}`, `{output}`, `{total}`, `{cacheRead}`, `{cacheWrite}`, `{context}`, `{contextMax}`, `{contextPercent}`, `{cost}`, `{usageLine}`, and `{session}`.
- Built-in `/usage full` is enriched to include cumulative context usage, model label, session key, and estimated cost when available.
- New inherited config: `agents.defaults.responseUsage` (`off` | `tokens` | `full`). Sessions without an explicit override inherit this default.
- Session-level explicit `responseUsage: "off"` is preserved even when a global default exists. `PATCH responseUsage: null` still clears the explicit override and returns the session to inherited behavior.
- When both `/usage` and `messages.responseFooter` are enabled:
  - static footers compose with built-in usage into one appended footer block
  - usage-aware custom footers suppress the separate built-in usage line
  - cost-only custom footers still compose with built-in usage unless `{usageLine}` is explicitly included
- In successful block-streaming flows, usage/footer text can still be emitted as a trailing payload even when the final body payload is intentionally suppressed.
- For streamed prefixes, unresolved late-bound usage/context/cost placeholders are blanked instead of leaking raw `{cost}`-style tokens.
- Added `docs/response-footer.md` documenting config, placeholders, composition rules, and examples.

## Diagram (if applicable)

For UI changes or non-trivial logic flows, include a small ASCII diagram reviewers can scan quickly. Otherwise write `N/A`.

```text
Before:
[user message]
  -> [reply generated]
  -> [optional responsePrefix applied]
  -> [optional /usage line appended late]
  -> [block streaming may suppress final payloads]
  -> [custom footer unavailable natively, or usage/footer can be lost or duplicated]

After:
[user message]
  -> [reply generated]
  -> [early-bound prefix resolved]
  -> [late-bound usage/context/cost/session template context resolved]
  -> [built-in usage line formatted]
  -> [responseFooter composed with or against usage]
  -> [footer block appended to last text payload or emitted as trailing payload]
  -> [reply sent without duplicate footer lines]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: Node/TypeScript repo snapshot in an offline CAAS container
- Model/provider: mocked reply-runner and gateway paths with synthetic provider/model/usage metadata
- Integration/channel (if any): normal reply pipeline, successful block-streaming reply pipeline, gateway session patch path, and TUI `/usage` toggle path; no live external channel in this container
- Relevant config (redacted):

```json
{
  "agents": {
    "defaults": {
      "responseUsage": "full"
    },
    "list": [{ "id": "main", "identity": { "name": "Jarvis" } }]
  },
  "messages": {
    "responsePrefix": "*{identityName}:* {cost}",
    "responseFooter": "{usageLine}\n— {identityName}"
  }
}
```

### Steps

1. Configure `agents.defaults.responseUsage` and optionally configure `messages.responseFooter`.
2. Trigger a normal reply, a successful block-streaming reply, and one or more `/usage` mode changes (`off`, `tokens`, `full`).
3. Inspect the final reply payloads and the effective session `responseUsage` state.

### Expected

- Sessions without an explicit override inherit `agents.defaults.responseUsage`.
- `/usage full` includes per-message tokens plus cumulative context, model, session, and estimated cost when available.
- Static custom footers fold with built-in usage into one footer block, while usage-aware footers suppress the separate built-in usage line.
- Successful block streaming does not drop footer/usage output just because the final body payload was suppressed.
- Prefixes do not leak raw late-bound placeholders before values are known.

### Actual

- In the covered paths, inherited usage defaults, explicit `off`, and clear-to-inherit (`null`) semantics behave as expected.
- `/usage full` is enriched with context/model/session/cost data in the covered reply paths.
- Footer composition follows the intended static-vs-usage-aware rules, and successful block streaming still returns footer/usage content as a trailing payload.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Representative snippets from the targeted coverage for this branch:

```text
Enriched built-in /usage full:
  Usage: 12k in / 450 out · cache 2.0k cached / 300 new · ctx 45k/200k (23%) · model gpt-5.4 · session `agent:main:webchat:dm:123`
```

```text
Static footer composed with built-in usage:
  ok

  Usage: 12 in / 3 out
  — footer
```

```text
Usage-aware footer suppressing duplicate built-in usage:
  ok

  ↑12 ↓3
```

```text
Successful block streaming with final body suppressed:
  streamed chunk: Hello
  trailing payload returned: Usage: 12 in / 3 out · cache 4 cached / 2 new
```

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - reviewed the touched config/type/schema/help/label surfaces for `messages.responseFooter` and `agents.defaults.responseUsage`
  - reviewed the reply-runner composition path for normal replies and the success path where block streaming suppresses final body payloads
  - inspected the new resolver/composition tests for footer, usage, inheritance, and prefix late-binding behavior
  - ran `git diff --check`
  - parsed changed TypeScript files successfully with the TypeScript compiler API in the offline container
  - manually exercised targeted helper/runtime behavior for prefix soft-blanking, cost-only footer composition, and enriched full usage-line formatting
- Edge cases checked:
  - explicit `off` versus inherited default
  - `PATCH responseUsage: null` re-enables inheritance
  - static footer versus usage-aware footer versus cost-only footer composition
  - blank or whitespace-only footer templates emit nothing
  - unresolved footer placeholders remain literal, while unresolved late-bound prefix placeholders are blanked
  - successful block streaming can return only a trailing footer payload without prepending a verbose new-session notice
- What you did **not** verify:
  - full `pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test`
  - live Telegram/Discord/WhatsApp channel behavior
  - message-tool outbound send behavior (`#39913` remains intentionally out of scope)
  - screenshot-based verification in a running OpenClaw instance

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

No review conversations exist yet because this PR body is being prepared before the PR is opened. Once the PR is live, the author should update these checkboxes based on the actual review state.

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk:
  - Enabling both built-in `/usage` and `messages.responseFooter` could produce confusing duplicate footer lines.
  - Mitigation: Added explicit composition rules and tests that suppress the separate built-in usage line when the footer already consumes usage-aware placeholders.
- Risk:
  - Late-bound usage/context/cost values could be missing or stale when templates render.
  - Mitigation: The footer path resolves against late-bound reply/session data, prefers refreshed persisted session state when available, and soft-blanks unresolved late-bound prefix placeholders instead of leaking raw tokens.
- Risk:
  - Block-streaming channels may render the usage/footer as a separate trailing message rather than mutating the already-streamed body.
  - Mitigation: The implementation preserves delivery without trying to retroactively rewrite the streamed content, and dedicated reply-runner coverage locks in the suppressed-final-payload success path.
- Risk:

target.title: fix(memory): block symlink escapes in memory_get
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `memory_get` could accept a path that lexically stayed under the workspace or an allowed extra path, but escaped through an intermediate directory symlink.
- Why it matters: an agent using `memory_get` could be tricked into pulling content from outside the workspace or QMD memory boundary, which means normal memory lookups could unexpectedly surface unrelated local files instead of only the notes the user intended memory tools to access.
- What changed: resolve target files and allowed roots to real paths before reading, then reject reads whose canonical path falls outside the allowed root; applied to both builtin memory reads and QMD-backed reads.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no changes to memory search ranking, indexing behavior, prompt logic, or non-memory file access surfaces.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Memory / storage
- [x] API / contracts

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: the read path checks only validated the lexical path prefix and direct symlink files, but did not verify that the resolved real path still stayed inside the allowed workspace / extraPath / QMD collection root.
- Missing detection / guardrail: no regression test covered an intermediate directory symlink that escaped the allowed root.
- Contributing context (if known): builtin and QMD read paths each enforced path rules separately, so both needed the same canonical-path containment check.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.read-file.test.ts`
  - `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: reject `memory_get` reads when a path under `memory/`, an allowed extra path, or a QMD collection crosses the boundary through an intermediate directory symlink.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug lives in path resolution and file-read authorization, so direct read-path tests cover it without requiring full indexing or end-to-end setup.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing tests already covered direct symlink files; this PR extends coverage to directory symlink escapes.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

`memory_get` now rejects markdown paths that escape the allowed workspace / extra path / QMD collection through intermediate directory symlinks.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
memory_get("memory/linked/secret.md")
-> lexical prefix check passes
-> linked/ resolves outside workspace
-> file is read

After:
memory_get("memory/linked/secret.md")
-> lexical prefix check passes
-> realpath resolves outside allowed root
-> request fails with "path required"
```
## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `Yes`
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  - This narrows the readable file scope for `memory_get` to the canonical allowed roots and blocks previously unintended cross-boundary reads through directory symlinks.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local Node/Bun repo test runner
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): memory core / QMD
- Relevant config (redacted): default local test config

### Steps

1. Create a markdown file outside the workspace or outside an allowed extra path / QMD collection root.
2. Add an intermediate directory symlink inside `memory/`, an allowed extra path, or a QMD collection that points to that outside directory.
3. Run `memory_get` on the symlinked markdown path.

### Expected

- The read is rejected with `path required`.

### Actual

- Before this fix, the read could succeed because only the lexical path prefix was checked.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
  - builtin memory reads reject directory symlink escapes
  - extra-path memory reads reject nested directory symlink escapes
  - QMD reads reject directory symlink escapes
- Edge cases checked:
  - direct symlink-file rejection still holds
  - transient file disappearance path still returns empty text correctly after switching reads to canonical paths
- What you did **not** verify:
  - full `pnpm test`
  - full `pnpm check`

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: setups that used a directory symlink inside `memory/`, an allowed extra path, or a QMD collection as an implicit way to reach files outside the configured memory roots will now fail on `memory_get`.
  - Mitigation: supported outside-workspace reads should be configured explicitly through `memorySearch.extraPaths` or QMD collections; this change aligns runtime reads with the documented memory-root contract and adds regression coverage for the escaped-symlink case.

target.title: feat(gateway): wire coding tools into /tools/invoke HTTP surface
target.body:
> **Update 2026-06-17 — head `4fe6f873c9` (scope owner gate to built-in host-FS write tools; restacked on #85664 `d625c6de23`).** Mirrors #85664's read-side fix for `write`/`edit` and addresses the ClawSweeper plugin-fallback finding (`docs/gateway/tools-invoke-http-api.md` write section). Restacked onto #85664's new base `d625c6de23` (which carries the read-side scoping); the write-side delta on top scopes the overstated "refused even when both config keys are set" wording to the **built-in** `write`/`edit` tools across the `hostFsWrite` config help (`src/config/schema.help.ts`), the `hostFsWrite` member comment (`src/config/types.gateway.ts`), the `hostFsWrite` bullet in `docs/gateway/configuration-reference.md`, and the write section of `docs/gateway/tools-invoke-http-api.md` (prose, truth table, _Plugin name collision_ note). Per the resolver (`src/gateway/tool-resolution.ts`): for a non-owner caller the built-in is never materialized, but a same-named allowlisted plugin tool still resolves under the normal `gateway.tools.allow`/`deny` policy. Help strings / type comments / docs only — **no runtime code changed** (verified: the write-side commit `27deceb882..4fe6f873c9` touches only `src/config/schema.help.ts`, `src/config/types.gateway.ts`, and the two `docs/gateway/*.md` files — no `src/gateway/`, `src/security/`, `zod-schema.ts`, or `schema.labels.ts`), so the nine-case live proof below stands unchanged. Verified in the `node:22-bookworm-slim` linux container: `pnpm check:docs` (format clean, markdownlint 0 errors, MDX ok, 0 broken links) and `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/config/schema.help.quality.test.ts` (23/23) both green.

---

> **Update 2026-06-17 — head `61e6f29bed` (config-help owner-gate fix; resolves [P2]/[P3]).** Restacked onto #85664's new base `7f9876c2b2`. The only delta vs the last-reviewed head `661d6838c0` is **config help / type docs** (verified: `git diff 661d6838c0 61e6f29bed` touches only `src/config/schema.help.ts` +4 and `src/config/types.gateway.ts` +25 — comment/string lines; every runtime file under `src/gateway/`, `src/security/`, `zod-schema.ts`, `schema.labels.ts` is byte-identical): the `hostFsWrite` `FIELD_HELP` string and the `GatewayDirectInvokeOptIns` summary + `hostFsWrite` member comment now document the **owner/admin sender** gate (`senderIsOwner === true`) the resolver enforces, and the type summary is refreshed so it no longer calls `hostFsWrite` "future work". This directly resolves ClawSweeper **[P2]** (config help omits the owner gate) and **[P3]** (stale `GatewayDirectInvokeOptIns` summary). Because no runtime code changed, the nine-case live proof below stands unchanged. Verified in the `node:22-bookworm-slim` linux container: `schema.help.quality.test.ts` (23/23) and `pnpm check:docs` both green.

---

> **Update 2026-06-17 — head `661d6838c0` (docs owner-gate fix; resolves [P2]).** Restacked onto #85664's docs-updated read base `ca46eb9946`. The only delta vs the last-reviewed head `afe36ccf45` is **docs**: the `read` + `write`/`edit` truth tables in `docs/gateway/tools-invoke-http-api.md` and the `hostFsRead`/`hostFsWrite` bullets in `docs/gateway/configuration-reference.md` now document the **owner/admin sender** gate (`senderIsOwner === true`) the resolver enforces — directly resolving ClawSweeper's **[P2]** ("Document the owner gate for file-tool opt-ins"). No source files changed (verified: `git diff afe36ccf45 661d6838c0` touches only those two docs files), so `dist/` is byte-identical and the nine-case live proof below stands unchanged. `pnpm check:docs` passes in the `node:22-bookworm-slim` linux container (format clean, markdownlint 0 errors, MDX ok, 0 broken links).

---

Builds on #85664. Narrow extension of the broader direct-invoke umbrella tracked in #37131.

## Summary

Extends #85664's narrow read-only landing to also expose `write` and `edit` over the gateway direct-invoke surfaces (HTTP `POST /tools/invoke` AND SDK RPC `tools.invoke`, which share the `resolveGatewayScopedTools` resolver). Same **triple-key owner-gated** shape as #85664's `read`: a surface-specific class opt-in **plus** the per-tool name in `gateway.tools.allow` **plus** `senderIsOwner === true`, all on the `http` surface.

## Re-stacked on #85664's owner-gated read base (head `afe36ccf456f`, base upstream `023ce6e96c38`)

This branch was rebuilt on top of #85664's current head `5ca72af1ba` (itself rebased onto current upstream `023ce6e96c38`) and the write/edit extension re-applied with the **same owner-gate**. This directly resolves the prior ClawSweeper [P1].

**Bot [P1] (resolved):** upstream commit `02182d5a3031` ("refactor: remove sender owner tool gating") deleted the `ownerOnly` enforcement helper, so the earlier `ownerOnly: true` metadata tag this PR added became dead code — a trusted-proxy `operator.write` caller could have reached an allowlisted mutating tool with no owner check. **Fix:** this PR no longer relies on that tag. `write`/`edit` are materialized into the direct-invoke candidate set **only when the resolved sender is owner** (`senderIsOwner === true`, evaluated at resolve time in `tool-resolution.ts`), exactly mirroring #85664's read gate. A trusted-proxy `operator.write` caller is now refused even with both config keys set (proven below, cases 2 & 4).

## Triple-key gating

To reach a write-class tool on the direct-invoke surface, ALL must hold:

| Key | Purpose | Default |
|---|---|---|
| `gateway.tools.directInvoke.hostFsWrite: true` | Class-level opt-in for the whole write family. | `false` |
| `gateway.tools.allow: [...]` includes the tool name | Per-tool enablement. | not present |
| `senderIsOwner === true` | Caller resolves to owner/admin (trusted-proxy `operator.admin`, or shared-secret bearer). | — |

Truth table (tool name `T` ∈ `{write, edit}`, surface = `http`):

| `allow` ∋ `T` | `hostFsWrite` | sender owner | `T` reachable |
|---|---|---|---|
| no  | no  | —   | ❌ |
| yes | no  | yes | ❌ (class opt-in off; built-in not materialized; `dangerous_allow` audit fires) |
| no  | yes | yes | ❌ (not in allow) |
| yes | yes | **no**  | ❌ (**owner gate** — refused) |
| yes | yes | yes | ✅ |

## Scope

- **Adds:** `write`, `edit` to direct-invoke (behind `hostFsWrite: true` + allow + owner).
- **Inherits from #85664:** `read` via `hostFsRead: true` + allow + owner.
- **`apply_patch`:** present in `DEFAULT_GATEWAY_HTTP_TOOL_DENY` for defense-in-depth, but the coding-tools factory produces no `apply_patch` entry, so it is never materialized (proven below, case 7).
- **NOT exposed:** `exec`, `process`, `spawn`, `shell` — RCE-class; out of scope for this PR.

## Audit

- `gateway.tools_invoke_http.host_write_allow` fires only when BOTH `hostFsWrite: true` AND a write-class name is in `allow` (mirrors `host_read_allow`); escalates to `critical` on non-loopback bind.
- `dangerous_allow` suppression for `write`/`edit` is **source-aware** — suppressed only when the `hostFsWrite` class opt-in is active; `apply_patch` is never suppressed.

## Files changed

- `src/config/types.gateway.ts` — adds `hostFsWrite?: boolean` to `GatewayDirectInvokeOptIns`
- `src/config/zod-schema.ts` — `hostFsWrite` schema entry
- `src/config/schema.help.ts`, `src/config/schema.labels.ts` — field help + label
- `src/security/dangerous-tools.ts` — adds `"write"`, `"edit"` to `DEFAULT_GATEWAY_HTTP_TOOL_DENY`
- `src/gateway/tool-resolution.ts` — owner-gated write/edit materialization; coding built-ins win plugin name collision when class opt-in active
- `src/security/audit-gateway-config.ts` — `host_write_allow` finding; source-aware `dangerous_allow` suppression
- `src/gateway/tools-invoke-http.test.ts` — write coding-tool collision precedence (owner→built-in, non-owner→plugin, opt-in-off→plugin)
- `src/security/audit-gateway-tools-http.test.ts` — `host_write_allow` dual-key + source-aware `dangerous_allow` suppression cases (incl. `apply_patch` never suppressed)
- `src/agents/agent-tools.ts` — coding-tool factory plumbing
- `docs/gateway/configuration-reference.md`, `docs/gateway/tools-invoke-http-api.md`, `docs/gateway/security/audit-checks.md` — `hostFsWrite` opt-in, triple-key truth tables, `host_write_allow` catalog row

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Extend #85664's owner-gated direct-invoke `read` to also expose `write` and `edit` over the Gateway HTTP `POST /tools/invoke` and SDK `tools.invoke` surfaces, behind the same triple key (class opt-in `directInvoke.hostFsWrite` + per-tool `gateway.tools.allow` + `senderIsOwner`). Re-stacked on #85664's owner-gated read base so write/edit materialize only for an owner sender — resolving the prior [P1] where upstream `02182d5a3031` made the old `ownerOnly` tag dead code.

**Real environment tested:** Built fresh from head `afe36ccf456f` inside a `node:22-bookworm-slim` arm64-native container (full runtime build → `dist/index.js`; SDK → `packages/sdk/dist/index.mjs`). Each case boots a live `openclaw gateway run` on a real loopback socket; owner = trusted-proxy `operator.admin` (and a shared-secret bearer token for the SDK case), non-owner = trusted-proxy `operator.write`. Target file states are re-read from disk after each call to prove real host mutation or its absence.

**Exact steps or command run after the patch:** `PROOF_HEAD=afe36ccf456f bash .proof-63919.sh` inside the container — boots nine gateway configs and dispatches `read`/`write`/`edit`/`apply_patch` over HTTP `/tools/invoke` and SDK `tools.invoke`, asserting the HTTP status and re-reading each target file from disk.

**Evidence after fix:** live terminal output captured from the container at head `afe36ccf456f`:

```
=== HEAD: afe36ccf456f ===

### LIVE PROOF (HTTP trusted-proxy surface) ###

--- 1 W-OWNER-ENABLED (allow=[write] hostFsWrite=true, operator.admin, tool=write) ---
  [HTTP operator.admin] 200 {"ok":true,"result":{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Successfully wrote 24 bytes to /work/.proof63/w1.txt"}]}}
  file=OWNER-WROTE-afe36ccf456f
--- 2 W-NONOWNER-DENIED (allow=[write] hostFsWrite=true, operator.write, tool=write) ---
  [HTTP operator.write] 404 {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: write"}}
  file=PRESEED
--- 3 E-OWNER-ENABLED (allow=[edit] hostFsWrite=true, operator.admin, tool=edit) ---
  [HTTP operator.admin] 200 {"ok":true,"result":{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Successfully replaced 1 block(s) in /work/.proof63/e3.txt."}],"details":{"diff":" 1 ALPHA\\n-2 BETA\\n+2 GAMMA-afe36ccf456f", ...}}}
  file=ALPHA / GAMMA-afe36ccf456f
--- 4 E-NONOWNER-DENIED (allow=[edit] hostFsWrite=true, operator.write, tool=edit) ---
  [HTTP operator.write] 404 {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: edit"}}
  file=ALPHA / BETA  (unchanged)
--- 5 W-DENIED-no-opt-in (allow=[write] hostFsWrite=false, operator.admin, tool=write) ---
  [HTTP operator.admin] 404 {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: write"}}
  file=<absent>
--- 6 W-DENIED-no-allow (allow=[] hostFsWrite=true, operator.admin, tool=write) ---
  [HTTP operator.admin] 404 {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: write"}}
  file=<absent>
--- 7 APPLYPATCH-denied (allow=[apply_patch] hostFsWrite=true, operator.admin, tool=apply_patch) ---
  [HTTP operator.admin] 404 {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: apply_patch"}}
--- 8 READ-REGRESSION (allow=[read] hostFsRead=true, operator.admin, tool=read) ---
  [HTTP operator.admin] 200 {"ok":true,"result":{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"READ-BASE-OK-afe36ccf456f\\n"}]}}

### LIVE PROOF (SDK token surface — shared-secret bearer = owner) ###

--- 9 SDK-W-OWNER (allow=[write] hostFsWrite=true, token bearer, tool=write) ---
  [SDK token] {"ok":true,"toolName":"write","output":{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Successfully wrote 28 bytes to /work/.proof63/w9.txt"}]},"source":"core"}
  file=SDK-OWNER-WROTE-afe36ccf456f

=== PROOF COMPLETE: HEAD=afe36ccf456f ===
```

Independent re-read of persisted file states after the run: `w1.txt`=`OWNER-WROTE-afe36ccf456f`, `w2.txt`=`PRESEED` (non-owner write left it untouched), `e3.txt`=`ALPHA/GAMMA-afe36ccf456f`, `e4.txt`=`ALPHA/BETA` (non-owner edit left it untouched), `w5.txt` and `w6.txt` absent (never created), `w9.txt`=`SDK-OWNER-WROTE-afe36ccf456f`.

**Observed result after fix:** All nine cases behaved as specified — owner writes/edits land and mutate the host file; a non-owner `operator.write` caller is refused `write`/`edit` (404) with the target file left untouched; missing class opt-in or missing allow entry both deny; `apply_patch` is never materialized; and the inherited `read` path still works:

| # | Case | Config | Caller | HTTP/SDK | File state |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | write owner | allow=[write], hostFsWrite=true | `operator.admin` | 200 wrote 24 bytes | written |
| 2 | write **non-owner** | allow=[write], hostFsWrite=true | `operator.write` | 404 not_found | **unchanged** (owner gate) |
| 3 | edit owner | allow=[edit], hostFsWrite=true | `operator.admin` | 200 replaced 1 block | edited |
| 4 | edit **non-owner** | allow=[edit], hostFsWrite=true | `operator.write` | 404 not_found | **unchanged** (owner gate) |
| 5 | write no opt-in | allow=[write], hostFsWrite=false | `operator.admin` | 404 not_found | never created |
| 6 | write no allow | allow=[], hostFsWrite=true | `operator.admin` | 404 not_found | never created |
| 7 | apply_patch | allow=[apply_patch], hostFsWrite=true | `operator.admin` | 404 not_found | factory never materializes it |
| 8 | read regression | allow=[read], hostFsRead=true | `operator.admin` | 200 + contents | #85664 base intact |
| 9 | write owner (SDK) | allow=[write], hostFsWrite=true | token bearer | ok=true wrote 28 bytes | written |

Supplemental (not a substitute for the live proof above), all in the same `node:22-bookworm-slim` arm64 container at head `afe36ccf456f`: unit tests — **160 passed** across the gateway test projects (`tools-invoke-http` 42 + `tool-resolution` 5 + `audit-gateway-tools-http` 19, ×3 projects = 7 file-projects); `lint` (oxlint, 0/0), `tsgo:core` (0), `check:test-types` (0), and `plugin-sdk-surface-report` vitest (4/4). **`checks-node-core-fast` / plugin-SDK surface budget:** this PR adds `hostFsWrite?` as a *field* on the existing `GatewayDirectInvokeOptIns` type — **zero new top-level exports** — so `plugin-sdk-surface-report --check` passes on the `+1` budget already carried by #85664's base commit (`config-types` 416 / public 10285 / public-fn 5164 / deprecated 3238). No further budget change in this PR.

**Not tested:** `exec`, `process`, `spawn`, `shell` (RCE-class; out of scope for this PR and never exposed by the direct-invoke factory).

target.title: feat: cute GTK-native Linux App (#75)
target.body: ## Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: - Problem: - OpenClaw did not have an in-repo native Linux companion app, so Linux users were missing a first-class desktop surface for gateway lifecycle, diagnostics, tray/status behavior, onboarding, and key operational management flows. - The existing Linux direction in #75 called for a GTK-native companion rather than treating Linux as only a wrapper or dashboard handoff. - Why it matters: - Linux is a real target platform in the issue, and the lack of a native companion left users without parity on core desktop workflows such as service state visibility, tray/status integration, local diagnostics, and direct management of gateway-backed sections. - Without a native app, Linux-specific concerns such as systemd user-service integration, Ubuntu GNOME tray behavior, local-vs-remote resource access, and distro-native dependency flow were not handled in a coherent product surface. - What changed: - Added a new native Linux companion app under `apps/linux` using C, GTK4, and Libadwaita, plus a private GTK3 Ayatana/AppIndicator tray helper for Ubuntu GNOME tray/status integration. - Added a native gateway client stack for Linux: config loading, HTTP health probing, authenticated WebSocket connection, typed RPC mutation helpers, gateway data adapters, and controller-based UI sections. - Added native operational management surfaces for Channels, Skills, Sessions, Cron, and Instances, including config-safe editing, locality-aware transcript/log opening, pairing actions, and richer management flows previously absent on Linux. - Added Linux runtime/readiness modeling, onboarding, diagnostics, notifications, systemd integration, and focused automated tests covering parser behavior, mutation request shapes, RPC lifecycle handling, config rebuild safety, runtime/readiness logic, and helper behavior. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): - This PR does not add distro packaging artifacts such as `.deb`, `.rpm`, or Snap packages. - This PR does not attempt to solve the Windows side. - This PR does not claim full macOS parity across every desktop capability; it establishes the native Linux companion foundation and a substantial in-app management surface. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [x] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [x] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [x] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [x] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [x] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [x] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Related #75 - [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable) For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`. - Root cause: - N/A - Missing detection / guardrail: - N/A - Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): - N/A - Why this regressed now: - N/A - If unknown, what was ruled out: - N/A ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should have caught this. Otherwise write `N/A`. - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [ ] Unit test - [ ] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [x] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: - N/A - Scenario the test should lock in: - N/A - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: - N/A - Existing test that already covers this (if any): - N/A - If no new test is added, why not: - N/A ## User-visible / Behavior Changes List user-visible changes (including defaults/config). If none, write `None`. - Linux now has a native GTK4 + Libadwaita companion app in-repo. - Ubuntu GNOME users get tray/status integration through the companion plus the private AppIndicator helper. - The Linux app can discover and work with the user `openclaw-gateway.service` lifecycle through native systemd integration. - The app surfaces first-run progression clearly across the main readiness states: - before `openclaw setup` - after `openclaw setup` but before gateway install - after gateway install with the service active and the gateway answering - The app surfaces runtime/readiness/onboarding/diagnostics states directly in the Linux UI. - Channels can now be inspected and managed in-app, including probe, logout, config editing, and WhatsApp QR linking flow. - Skills can now be filtered and managed in-app, including enable/disable, install, and env/API-key updates. - Sessions can now be managed in-app, including patch/reset/compact/delete and log access. - Cron can now be managed in-app, including scheduler status, recent runs, create/edit, run-now, enable/disable, delete, and transcript access. - Instances now expose richer remote node details, pending pairing approval/reject flows, and copy-debug-info behavior. - Log/transcript opening is locality-aware: local resources open locally when available, otherwise the UI falls back safely. - Channel config editing now validates edited JSON, preserves unrelated config keys, and saves using `baseHash`. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text Before: [user on Linux] -> [CLI / dashboard / partial prototype paths] -> [limited lifecycle visibility and little or no native management] After: [user on Linux] -> [native GTK4 companion app] -> [systemd + HTTP + authenticated WebSocket runtime model] -> [tray / diagnostics / onboarding / management sections] -> [native actions for channels, skills, sessions, cron, instances] ```` ## Security Impact (required) * New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: * The Linux companion introduces a new native desktop surface that can read local runtime context, inspect service state, connect to the local gateway over HTTP/WebSocket, edit config, open local logs/transcripts when available, and trigger gateway-backed management mutations. * Risk is mitigated by keeping the runtime model explicit, separating systemd discovery from gateway RPC transport, validating config JSON before save, preserving unrelated config content during rebuild, using `baseHash` on config writes, hardening request shapes through typed mutation helpers, and covering lifecycle/parsing/request-shape behavior in targeted tests. * Local-vs-remote resource access is handled explicitly so the app does not blindly assume every gateway-returned path is locally openable. ## Repro + Verification ### Environment * OS: * Ubuntu 24.04 and Ubuntu 26.04 GNOME * Runtime/container: * Bare-metal / local user-session workflow with systemd user service * Model/provider: * Local gateway-backed companion flow; model/provider depends on gateway config * Integration/channel (if any): * systemd user service, HTTP health, authenticated gateway WebSocket, GTK4 + Libadwaita, Ayatana/AppIndicator * Relevant config (redacted): * Fresh local OpenClaw config and user `openclaw-gateway.service`; secrets redacted ### Steps 1. Install Linux build dependencies and clone/build OpenClaw. 2. Build and run the Linux app with Meson. 3. Launch the Linux companion app before running `openclaw setup`. 4. Run `openclaw setup`, then relaunch or refresh the companion app. 5. Install and start the user gateway service. 6. Launch or refresh the Linux companion app again. 7. Exercise tray/status, diagnostics, onboarding/readiness, and the Dashboard / General / Channels / Skills / Sessions / Cron / Instances sections. 8. Run the Linux test suite. ### Expected * Before setup, the app reports the missing setup/runtime state clearly. * After setup but before gateway install, the app reports configured-but-not-installed service state clearly. * After gateway install/start, the app reports a healthy connected state, with diagnostics, dashboard, and general surfaces reflecting the running gateway. * The Linux app builds, launches, connects through the native runtime model, reflects systemd/gateway state accurately, and exposes native management flows for the major gateway-backed sections. * The Linux test suite passes. ### Actual * Matches expected in local verification. ## Evidence Attach at least one: * [x] Failing test/log before + passing after * [x] Trace/log snippets * [x] Screenshot/recording * [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) What I personally verified (not just CI), and how: * Verified scenarios: * Built the Linux app from a clean checkout on Ubuntu 24.04 and Ubuntu 26.04 GNOME. * Ran the Meson test suite locally. * Verified tray helper spawn and tray/status behavior. * Verified systemd user-service discovery and lifecycle reflection. * Verified diagnostics/readiness/runtime presentation. * Verified first-run progression manually with screenshots: * app launched before `openclaw setup` * app after `openclaw setup` * app after gateway install/start with the gateway answering properly * Verified the healthy connected state manually against a fresh configured local gateway. * Verified additional Linux app surfaces manually, including Dashboard, General, and Diagnostics. * Verified the main Linux companion window and controller-backed sections load and refresh. * Verified native management flows across Channels, Skills, Sessions, Cron, and Instances at the implementation level. * Verified config editing flow behavior, including full-document rebuild and hash-aware save path. * Verified locality-aware log/transcript fallback behavior. * Edge cases checked: * Invalid config JSON aborts save. * Config rebuild preserves unrelated top-level keys and sibling channel config. * Mutation request shapes match the intended backend contract structure. * RPC lifecycle handling covers success/error/timeout/disconnect cleanup paths. * Tray-helper lifecycle and cleanup behavior were tightened. * Readiness states remain intelligible across the bootstrap sequence from no setup -> configured -> installed/running. * What I did **not** verify: * Official distro packaging artifacts (`.deb`, `.rpm`, Snap) are not included here. * Broad desktop-environment compatibility beyond the Ubuntu GNOME target path. * Full end-to-end parity for every macOS companion capability. ## Review Conversations * [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. * [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. ## Compatibility / Migration * Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) * No * Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) * No * If yes, exact upgrade steps: * N/A ## Risks and Mitigations List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`. * Risk: * Linux desktop behavior can vary by distro and shell, especially around tray hosting. * Mitigation: * Target the initial implementation clearly at Ubuntu GNOME, use a private AppIndicator helper, and keep the helper boundary isolated from the GTK4 main process. * Risk: * The Linux companion now depends on a larger native runtime stack: systemd context, HTTP health, WebSocket RPC, and gateway payload parsing. * Mitigation: * Keep those concerns separated into dedicated modules and cover parser/RPC/mutation behavior with focused tests. * Risk: * Config editing could accidentally overwrite unrelated config content if the save path is too narrow. * Mitigation: * Load the full config document, edit only the relevant subtree, rebuild the full document, validate JSON before save, and send `baseHash` with `config.set`. * Risk: * Transcript/log opening can fail or behave incorrectly if the gateway resource is not local to the GTK host. * Mitigation: * Enforce locality-aware fallback logic and only open local resources directly when they are actually present and accessible. * Risk: * This is a large first-class app addition and therefore a large review surface. * Mitigation: * Keep the app organized into focused subsystems and section controllers, provide a documented Linux build/test path, and include targeted tests for the most contract-sensitive areas.

target.title: fix(telegram): clarify inbound document fallback reason
target.body:
## Summary

* Problem: Inbound Telegram document download failures could degrade into a placeholder-only body like `<media:document>` with no explanation.
* Why it matters: This makes attachment failures ambiguous and harder for the agent to interpret, especially for Bot API size-limit cases.
* What changed: Preserve `<media:document>` for failed inbound document downloads and append an explicit reason line for `file too large` vs `download failed`.
* What did NOT change (scope boundary): This PR does not add support for downloading Telegram files over the Bot API size limit and does not change proxy, timeout, SSRF, or unrelated media behavior.

## Change Type (select all)

* [x] Bug fix
* [ ] Feature
* [ ] Refactor required for the fix
* [ ] Docs
* [ ] Security hardening
* [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

* [ ] Gateway / orchestration
* [ ] Skills / tool execution
* [ ] Auth / tokens
* [ ] Memory / storage
* [x] Integrations
* [ ] API / contracts
* [ ] UI / DX
* [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

* Related #55917
* [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

* Root cause: Failed inbound Telegram document downloads could fall back to placeholder-only output without surfacing why the attachment was unavailable.
* Missing detection / guardrail: Existing coverage did not lock in the final rendered body behavior for failed inbound document downloads.
* Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): Scoped to the Telegram inbound document fallback path discussed in #55917.
* Why this regressed now: The fallback path preserved message delivery, but not enough user-visible context to explain attachment unavailability.
* If unknown, what was ruled out: Ruled out broader Telegram transport, proxy, timeout, SSRF, and Bot API size-limit behavior changes as part of this fix.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

* Coverage level that should have caught this:

  * [x] Unit test
  * [x] Seam / integration test
  * [ ] End-to-end test
  * [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
* Target test or file:

  * `extensions/telegram/src/bot/delivery.resolve-media-retry.test.ts`
  * `extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-context.media-fallback.test.ts`
* Scenario the test should lock in:

  * successful document download path remains unchanged
  * failed document download returns an explicit fallback reason
  * file-too-large and generic download failure are distinguished
  * final rendered body keeps `<media:document>` and includes the unavailable reason
  * text + failed document preserves user text, placeholder, and reason
* Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The bug spans both resolver behavior and final body rendering, so focused resolver coverage plus one final body-format test is the smallest reliable guardrail.
* Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing retry coverage covered related resolver behavior, but not final body rendering for failed inbound document downloads.
* If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

* Failed inbound Telegram document downloads no longer degrade into a silent placeholder-only body.
* Failed inbound documents still preserve `<media:document>`.
* The final message body now includes one explicit reason line:

  * `Telegram attachment unavailable: file too large for Bot API download`
  * `Telegram attachment unavailable: download failed`
* Successful inbound document downloads remain unchanged.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[inbound Telegram document download fails] -> [body becomes <media:document>] -> [failure reason is unclear]

After:
[inbound Telegram document download fails]
  -> [keep <media:document>]
  -> [append explicit unavailable reason]
  -> [agent sees both document placeholder and failure reason]
```

## Security Impact (required)

* New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
* Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
* New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
* Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
* Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
* If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

* OS: Windows 10
* Runtime/container: local dev environment
* Model/provider: N/A
* Integration/channel (if any): Telegram extension logic
* Relevant config (redacted): standard local repo/test setup

### Steps

1. Receive an inbound Telegram document message.
2. Hit a document download failure in the existing fallback path.
3. Build the final message body for agent ingestion.

### Expected

* The final body keeps `<media:document>`.
* The final body includes an explicit unavailable reason.
* Successful document downloads remain unchanged.

### Actual

* Before this fix, failed inbound Telegram document downloads could degrade into a placeholder-only body such as `<media:document>` with no explicit reason.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

* [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
* [ ] Trace/log snippets
* [ ] Screenshot/recording
* [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

* Verified scenarios:

  * Reviewed the final reduced patch and confirmed it stays scoped to Telegram inbound document fallback messaging.
  * Ran focused local tests for resolver behavior and final body rendering.
* Edge cases checked:

  * successful document download path remains unchanged
  * file-too-large fallback reason
  * generic download-failed fallback reason
  * text + failed document preserves user text, placeholder, and reason
* What you did **not** verify:

  * live end-to-end Telegram bot reproduction

## Review Conversations

* [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
* [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

* Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
* Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
* Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
* If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

* Risk: The unavailable-reason plumbing now flows through Telegram message-context handling.

  * Mitigation: The patch is scoped to failed inbound document handling and covered by focused resolver/body tests.
* Risk: Final body formatting could accidentally drop user text or the placeholder.

  * Mitigation: Focused rendered-body coverage locks in both document-only and text + failed document output.

target.title: fix(ui): sync message delete to session transcript backend
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** Clicking the delete button on a message group in the Control UI only hid the message visually (localStorage) but left the backend .jsonl transcript untouched. The AI could still see the "deleted" content on the next turn.
- **Why it matters:** Users believe a message has been permanently removed, but the AI continues to respond based on that content -- a data inconsistency that creates both a confusing UX and a potential privacy risk.
- **What changed:** Added `sessions.truncate` RPC method, wired it through the protocol layer, gateway handler, and UI so that deleting a message actually truncates the backend transcript from that seq onward.
- **What did NOT change:** Existing `sessions.compact`, `sessions.reset`, or any other session mutation paths are untouched.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Security hardening

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Memory / storage
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX

## Linked Issue/PR

- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History

- **Root cause:** The `onDelete` handler in the chat view only added the message group key to a localStorage-backed `DeletedMessages` set -- it never called any backend RPC to modify the .jsonl transcript.
- **Missing detection / guardrail:** No integration test verified that deleting a message in the UI actually affected the transcript file.
- **Prior context:** The `sessions.compact` and `sessions.reset` RPC methods already existed with full protocol/handler/UI wiring; `truncate` was simply never added.
- **Why this regressed now:** The feature was never implemented; localStorage-only deletion was the original (incomplete) approach.

## Changes

### Backend (`src/gateway/server-methods/sessions.ts`)

Three-phase truncate with full safety:

1. **Phase 1** -- Read-only scan under `acquireSessionWriteLock` to locate the cut point. ENOENT-guarded. Does NOT abort active runs yet.
2. **Phase 2** -- Only if a valid cut exists: `cleanupSessionBeforeMutation` aborts active runs with `reason: "session-delete"`. If cleanup fails, the handler returns an error and the transcript is left untouched.
3. **Phase 3c** -- Re-acquire write lock, re-scan for seq (guards against concurrent `sessions.compact`), write-then-rename: writes truncated content to `.tmp.*`, renames original to `.bak.*`, renames temp to original. Preserves original file mode (`statSync(filePath).mode & 0o777`).

Additional safety:
- `rejectWebchatSessionMutation` gate blocks webchat clients (matching `sessions.patch`/`sessions.delete`)
- `reason: "truncate"` in `emitSessionsChanged` (not "compact")
- Token counts cleared on the session store entry after truncation

### Protocol layer

- `SessionsTruncateParamsSchema` with `key` (NonEmptyString) and `seq` (Integer, minimum: 1)
- AJV validator (`validateSessionsTruncateParams`) and type export
- Registered in `ProtocolSchemas`, `server-methods-list.ts`, and `method-scopes.ts` (operator.write)

### UI (`ui/src/ui/app-render.ts` + `ui/src/ui/views/chat.ts`)

- `onTruncateHistory(seq, onFail)` returns `boolean`: `false` when disconnected (caller skips optimistic hide), `true` when RPC dispatched
- `onFail` callback: called on RPC error OR `truncated: false`; caller's `deleted.restore(item.key)` reverses the optimistic hide
- Session-scoped: captures `state.sessionKey` at dispatch time; ignores `.then`/`.catch` if user navigated away
- Run-ID aware: captures `state.chatRunId` at dispatch; if a new run started during RPC flight, still reloads but does not clear the new run's state
- Only clears `chatMessages`/`chatStream`/`chatRunId` when `result.truncated === true`
- `.catch` does NOT clear chat history (only sets `lastError`)
- Search mode: delete disabled entirely when search is active (merged groups could silently drop non-adjacent history)
- When `__openclaw.seq` metadata is absent (e.g. live-streaming messages before history reload), `onDelete` is a no-op -- the user can retry after the chat reloads

### Swift models

- `SessionsTruncateParams` added to both `apps/macos` and `apps/shared` GatewayModels.swift

## Review Issues Addressed

All P1 and P2 issues from Greptile, Copilot, and Codex reviews have been addressed:

| # | Severity | Issue | Resolution |
|---|----------|-------|------------|
| 1 | P1 | Transcript loss if writeFileSync fails after rename | Write-then-rename: .tmp.* then .bak.* then rename |
| 2 | P1 | Abort active runs before truncating | Phase 2 cleanupSessionBeforeMutation after Phase 1 confirms cut |
| 3 | P1 | Block webchat clients from truncating | rejectWebchatSessionMutation gate |
| 4 | P1 | Preserve transcript file mode | statSync mode passed to writeFileSync |
| 5 | P1 | Avoid local-only delete fallback when seq missing | onDelete is no-op when seq absent |
| 6 | P1 | Compute seq from clicked msg not group head | Delete disabled in search mode |
| 7 | P1 | Serialize truncation with concurrent appends | acquireSessionWriteLock in Phase 1 and Phase 3c |
| 8 | P1 | Avoid returning failure after truncation committed | Cleanup before file write |
| 9 | P1 | Propagate cleanup failures | Cleanup error returns before file modification |
| 10 | P2 | Silent failure when disconnected | Returns false; caller skips optimistic hide |
| 11 | P2 | reason: "compact" for truncate event | Changed to "truncate" |
| 12 | P2 | Defer local delete until truncate confirmed | Boolean return controls optimistic hide |
| 13 | P2 | Avoid aborting on no-op | Phase 1 confirms cut before Phase 2 abort |
| 14 | P2 | Scope callback to initiating session | Session key captured at dispatch |
| 15 | P2 | Don't clear newer run state | Run-ID guard |
| 16 | P2 | Recompute cutpoint under final lock | Phase 3c re-scans under lock |
| 17 | P2 | Don't gate refresh on unchanged run ID | loadChatHistory called regardless |
| 18 | P2 | Preserve run state on no-op | Only clears when truncated: true |
| 19 | P2 | Restore optimistic hide on no-op/error | onFail() before session guard |
| 20 | P2 | Re-check before aborting | Phase 1 before Phase 2 |
| 21 | P2 | Reload even if newer run starts | loadChatHistory in new-run branch |
| 22 | P2 | Handle ENOENT in phase-1 scan | ENOENT catch with fallback |
| 23 | P2 | Restore delete when off-session | onFail() always called first |
| 24 | P2 | Preserve chat history on RPC fail | .catch only sets lastError |
| 25 | P2 | Comment refers to fromSeq | Updated to p.seq |

## Regression Test Plan

- All 30 method-scope classification tests pass
- All 37 protocol tests pass
- All 38 gateway sessions integration tests pass
- `pnpm build` passes cleanly
- `pnpm format` clean

target.title: fix(synology-chat): handle HEAD probe and return 200 on webhook ACK
target.body:
Fixes #53439

## Problem

Synology Chat bot webhooks fail silently in two ways, discovered through live debugging with a Synology NAS bot integration:

**1. HEAD probe**

Synology sends a `HEAD` request to the outgoing webhook URL before each `POST` to verify the endpoint is alive. This was observed in DSM's Synology Chat integration logs as a probe immediately preceding every message delivery attempt. OpenClaw returned `405 Method Not Allowed` for HEAD, causing Synology to mark the bot endpoint as unreachable and stop sending `POST` requests entirely.

**2. ACK response code**

After successfully accepting a message POST, OpenClaw responded with `204 No Content`. Synology Chat expects `200 OK` with `{"success":true}` in the body — this is what its own incoming webhook API returns on success. When it receives a 204, the Synology Chat UI shows:

> _(Only visible to you) Failed to send a request to the bot server. Please contact the bot owner._

After this error, Synology stops delivering subsequent messages from that conversation until the webhook URL is re-saved in the integration settings, resetting the failure state.

## Changes

- Respond `200 OK` to `HEAD` requests in `createWebhookHandler`
- Rename `respondNoContent` → `respondAck`, returning `200 OK` + `{"success":true}` to match Synology's expected response format
- Update all affected tests to expect `200`; add a new test covering the HEAD probe

## Testing

All 106 extension tests pass (`pnpm test:extension synology-chat`). Verified end-to-end on a Synology NAS running DSM 7 + Synology Chat bot integration — messages now deliver reliably without the "Failed" error, and the connection stays alive across multiple consecutive messages.

## AI disclosure

- [x] AI-assisted (GUPPI / Claude Sonnet 4.6 via OpenClaw)
- [x] Tested end-to-end on real hardware
- [x] Author understands the changes

target.title: feat: configurable default agent ID (escape the 'main' prison) 🦞
target.body:
## Summary

Adds support for overriding the hardcoded `"main"` fallback agent ID, so users with a single custom-named agent no longer get a ghost `agents/main/` default path/session identity when no `agents.list` is configured.

## The Problem

`DEFAULT_AGENT_ID` falls back to `"main"`. Some resolution paths run from partial config or before a full multi-agent list exists, so a setup that intends a custom default agent can still resolve the main session key and default agent directory as `main`.

## Solution

Two complementary override mechanisms:

### 1. Config key: `agents.defaultAgentId`

```json
{
  "agents": {
    "defaultAgentId": "maine-lobster"
  }
}
```

### 2. Env var: `OPENCLAW_DEFAULT_AGENT_ID`

```bash
export OPENCLAW_DEFAULT_AGENT_ID=maine-lobster
```

### Priority (highest → lowest)

1. `agents.list[].default: true` (explicit default agent)
2. `agents.list[0]` (first configured agent)
3. `agents.defaultAgentId` config key
4. `OPENCLAW_DEFAULT_AGENT_ID` env var
5. Hardcoded `"main"` (backward compatible)

## Changes

- Adds `agents.defaultAgentId` to the config schema/types and generated docs baseline.
- Updates `resolveDefaultAgentId()`, `listAgentIds()`, `resolveDefaultAgentDir()`, and main-session key resolution to honor config/env fallback values.
- Keeps populated `agents.list` behavior unchanged: list default/first entry still wins.
- Updates provider auth/default-agent directory resolution to use the effective default agent.
- Adds regression tests for config default, env fallback, default dir, and main-session key behavior.

## Testing

- `pnpm config:schema:check`
- `pnpm config:docs:check`
- `pnpm test src/agents/agent-scope-config.test.ts src/config/sessions/main-session.test.ts src/config/sessions/session-key.test.ts src/commands/auth-choice.test.ts src/commands/auth-choice.apply.plugin-provider.test.ts --maxWorkers=1`
- `pnpm check:changed`
- `git merge-tree --write-tree HEAD origin/main` → merge-clean

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: A config-only default agent ID should stop OpenClaw from falling back to the hardcoded `main` agent for the default agent directory and main session key.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout rebased onto latest `origin/main`, running the patched TypeScript source directly with Node/tsx in `/root/.openclaw/workspace/openclaw-pr-acpx/pr-51762`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```console
$ node --import tsx --input-type=module <<'EOF'
import { resolveDefaultAgentId, resolveDefaultAgentDir } from './src/agents/agent-scope-config.ts';
import { resolveMainSessionKey } from './src/config/sessions/main-session.ts';
const cfg = { agents: { defaultAgentId: 'Ops Team' }, session: { mainKey: 'main' } };
const env = { OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR: '/tmp/openclaw-real-proof' };
console.log('defaultAgentId=' + resolveDefaultAgentId(cfg, env));
console.log('defaultAgentDir=' + resolveDefaultAgentDir(cfg, env));
console.log('mainSessionKey=' + resolveMainSessionKey(cfg, env));
EOF
```

- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output copied from the patched real source command above:

```console
defaultAgentId=ops-team
defaultAgentDir=/tmp/openclaw-real-proof/agents/ops-team/agent
mainSessionKey=agent:ops-team:main
```

- **Observed result after fix**: `agents.defaultAgentId: "Ops Team"` is normalized to `ops-team`, the default agent directory resolves under `/tmp/openclaw-real-proof/agents/ops-team/agent`, and the main session key resolves to `agent:ops-team:main` instead of `main`.
- **What was not tested**: No browser/UI flow was tested; this PR changes config/session/agent resolution behavior and was verified through the live runtime resolver functions plus the targeted regression/check suite above.

Fixes #17477 🦞
