target.title: fix(agent): prevent sticky model fallback
target.body:
## Summary
- roll back temporary auto fallback model overrides after successful fallback attempts
- keep session routing pointed at the selected model while still recording usage/cost for the actual fallback model used
- update runner/session accounting coverage and add the Unreleased changelog entry

## Root cause
Successful fallback candidates were persisted into session override state but only rolled back on errors/classified failures. Usage persistence also wrote `providerUsed`/`modelUsed` into the session identity, so one fallback run could pin later turns to the smaller fallback model even after the primary model recovered.

## Validation
- `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/session.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.test.ts`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: A temporary model fallback should not make the session stick to the fallback model. After a primary-model rate limit sends one turn to a configured fallback, the next turn in the same session should try the configured primary again while usage/status still records the actual fallback runtime used for the fallback turn.

Real environment tested: Local source checkout at PR head `c4f5337a7fdcc9f11c17ff6f42d8e8295fb77eed`, Node `v24.15.0`, pnpm `11.1.0`, isolated `OPENCLAW_HOME=/var/tmp/openclaw-pr75270-proof/home`, isolated workspace `/var/tmp/openclaw-pr75270-proof/workspace`, and a local OpenAI-compatible HTTP endpoint on `127.0.0.1`. No real user session, live channel, or production OpenClaw home was used.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: 1. Configured `agents.defaults.model.primary` as `primary-local/primary-model` with `fallbacks: ["fallback-local/fallback-model"]`.
2. Started a local OpenAI-compatible `/v1/chat/completions` server that returns four HTTP 429 responses for `primary-model`, then returns streaming success for `fallback-model`; after that, `primary-model` returns streaming success.
3. Ran two local embedded agent turns against the same session id:

`/usr/bin/node openclaw.mjs agent --local --agent main --session-id pr75270-proof --thinking off --json --timeout 120 --message <proof message>`

Evidence after fix: Copied terminal output from `/var/tmp/openclaw-pr75270-proof/proof-output.json`:

```json
{
  "first": {
    "exitCode": 0,
    "text": "PR75270 fallback proof OK",
    "provider": "fallback-local",
    "model": "fallback-model",
    "fallbackAttempts": [
      {
        "provider": "primary-local",
        "model": "primary-model",
        "error": "429 proof primary rate limit",
        "reason": "rate_limit",
        "status": 429
      }
    ],
    "requestModels": [
      "primary-model",
      "primary-model",
      "primary-model",
      "primary-model",
      "fallback-model",
      "fallback-model"
    ],
    "storeAfterTurn": {
      "modelProvider": "fallback-local",
      "model": "fallback-model",
      "providerOverride": null,
      "modelOverride": null,
      "modelOverrideSource": null,
      "fallbackNoticeSelectedModel": null,
      "fallbackNoticeActiveModel": null
    }
  },
  "second": {
    "exitCode": 0,
    "text": "PR75270 primary recovered OK",
    "provider": "primary-local",
    "model": "primary-model",
    "fallbackAttempts": [],
    "requestModels": [
      "primary-model",
      "primary-model"
    ],
    "storeAfterTurn": {
      "modelProvider": "primary-local",
      "model": "primary-model",
      "providerOverride": null,
      "modelOverride": null,
      "modelOverrideSource": null,
      "fallbackNoticeSelectedModel": null,
      "fallbackNoticeActiveModel": null
    }
  },
  "allRequestModels": [
    "primary-model",
    "primary-model",
    "primary-model",
    "primary-model",
    "fallback-model",
    "fallback-model",
    "primary-model",
    "primary-model"
  ]
}
```

The runtime log also showed the fallback decision on the first turn:

```text
[model-fallback/decision] model fallback decision: decision=candidate_failed requested=primary-local/primary-model candidate=primary-local/primary-model reason=rate_limit next=fallback-local/fallback-model detail=429 proof primary rate limit
[model-fallback/decision] model fallback decision: decision=candidate_succeeded requested=primary-local/primary-model candidate=fallback-local/fallback-model reason=unknown next=none
```

Observed result after fix: The first turn used the configured fallback after the synthetic primary returned 429, and `fallbackAttempts` recorded the primary failure. The same session's persisted auto override fields were clear after the fallback turn. The second turn, with the primary endpoint recovered, requested and completed on `primary-local/primary-model` with no fallback attempts. This verifies the session did not stay pinned to the fallback model.

What was not tested: This proof used a local OpenAI-compatible endpoint rather than a paid hosted provider, did not exercise real Telegram/WhatsApp delivery, and did not test provider-specific OAuth rotation. Those paths are covered separately by the automated tests listed above.

target.title: feat(agents): add description field for dynamic agent discovery
target.body:
## Summary

Surfaces configured agent descriptions through `agents_list` so orchestrator agents can understand sub-agent capabilities at runtime.

This was originally requested in #7490. Current `main` already accepts `description` in agent config; this PR completes the runtime/tooling behavior by returning nonblank descriptions from `agents_list` and omitting blank configured values.

## Changes

- Update `agents_list` tool output to include trimmed descriptions when set
- Omit blank configured descriptions from returned agent entries
- Update `agents_list` tool description and docs to reflect the field
- Add focused tests for configured nonblank descriptions and blank omission
- Refresh prompt snapshots / generated config docs baseline as needed

## Use Case

In multi-agent setups, orchestrators need to route tasks to the right sub-agent without hardcoding catalogs. With this change:

```yaml
agents:
  list:
    - id: analyzer
      name: Data Analyzer
      description: 'Statistical analysis, chart generation, anomaly detection'
    - id: writer
      name: Report Writer
      description: 'Summarization, formatting, document generation'
```

`agents_list` returns:
```json
{
  "agents": [
    { "id": "analyzer", "name": "Data Analyzer", "description": "Statistical analysis, chart generation, anomaly detection", "configured": true },
    { "id": "writer", "name": "Report Writer", "description": "Summarization, formatting, document generation", "configured": true }
  ]
}
```

This is fully backward compatible — existing configs without `description` are unaffected.

Closes #7490

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: `agents_list` now returns configured nonblank `agents.list[].description` values and omits blank configured descriptions.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw Gateway built from this PR branch at head `ea4113c4c2`, isolated `OPENCLAW_HOME=/tmp/openclaw-pr75225-proof`, loopback Gateway `127.0.0.1:19977`, plugins disabled, no production Shannon runtime touched.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```sh
OPENCLAW_HOME=/tmp/openclaw-pr75225-proof \
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-pr75225-proof/.openclaw \
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-pr75225-proof/.openclaw/openclaw.json \
node openclaw.mjs --log-level info gateway --port 19977 --bind loopback

curl -sS http://127.0.0.1:19977/tools/invoke \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -H 'x-openclaw-scopes: operator.write' \
  -d '{"tool":"agents_list","action":"json","args":{},"sessionKey":"agent:main:proof"}'
```

  Proof config: requester `main` has `subagents.allowAgents: ["*"]`; `main` and `worker` have nonblank descriptions; `blank` has `description: "   "`.

- Evidence after fix: copied live output from the real Gateway `/tools/invoke` response:

```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "result": {
    "details": {
      "requester": "main",
      "allowAny": true,
      "agents": [
        {
          "id": "main",
          "name": "Main Proof",
          "configured": true,
          "agentRuntime": { "id": "codex", "source": "implicit" },
          "description": "Primary proof agent description should appear in agents_list."
        },
        {
          "id": "blank",
          "name": "Blank Proof",
          "configured": true,
          "agentRuntime": { "id": "codex", "source": "implicit" }
        },
        {
          "id": "worker",
          "name": "Worker Proof",
          "configured": true,
          "agentRuntime": { "id": "codex", "source": "implicit" },
          "description": "Handles focused backend proof tasks."
        }
      ]
    }
  }
}
```

- Observed result after fix: nonblank descriptions are present for `main` and `worker`; the blank configured description for `blank` is omitted from the returned entry.
- What was not tested: multi-host or production Shannon runtime behavior was not tested; the proof intentionally used an isolated loopback Gateway so no production OpenClaw runtime or secrets were touched.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: this is a local Gateway proof, not a full fleet proof. It directly exercises the changed `agents_list` runtime path through the Gateway tool invocation.

## Tests and validation

Additional validation on the same rebased head:

```sh
pnpm build
./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check src/agents/tools/agents-list-tool.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.agents.test.ts src/agents/tools/agents-list-tool.test.ts docs/tools/subagents.md docs/gateway/config-agents.md
./node_modules/.bin/vitest run src/agents/openclaw-tools.agents.test.ts src/agents/tools/agents-list-tool.test.ts
pnpm prompt:snapshots:check
pnpm config:docs:check
```

Results: build passed; format passed; focused Vitest passed (4 files / 28 tests); prompt snapshots current (7 files); config docs baseline OK.

target.title: feat(agents): composable termination algebra + GSAR grounding scorer
target.body:
Closes #77981

## What this does

Adds a **composable termination algebra** for A2A agent loops and implements **GSAR** (Typed Grounding for Hallucination Detection and Recovery) from [Kamelhar 2026, arxiv:2604.23366](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23366) as a first-class `TerminationCondition`. Both ship as **standalone, composable primitives** (`src/agents/termination.ts`, `src/agents/gsar.ts`) with full unit + live-API coverage.

> **Scope (updated):** this PR is **library/groundwork only**. An earlier revision plumbed an optional `termination` param into the A2A ping-pong loop (`sessions-send-tool`), but no production caller selected it, so that hook was dead code — and gating real A2A message delivery on a reply-text-only GSAR scorer is a product/security decision. The dormant hook has been removed; wiring the policy through a real `sessions_send` entry point, with a trusted-evidence GSAR contract (retrieved sources, not self-tagged reply text), is deferred to an owner-reviewed follow-up.

---

## Proof 1 — the algebra saves turns

```
Task: "Where is the nearest coffee shop?"

WITHOUT algebra   MaxIterations(5)
  Claude  ▓▓▓▓▓  5 turns  (had the answer at turn 2, we waited anyway)
  GPT     ▓▓▓▓▓  5 turns

WITH algebra   TextMention("FOUND IT").or(MaxIterations(5))
  Claude  ▓▓░░░  2 turns  ← "Blue Bottle on Mission St. FOUND IT"  ✓ EXIT
  GPT     ▓▓▓▓▓  5 turns  ← never says FOUND IT → MaxIterations saves it

  saved: 3 turns = 60% less waiting for Claude
```

Claude naturally signals completion. GPT does not. The combinator captures this difference without any provider-specific code.

---

## Proof 2 — soft signals must be paired with a hard bound

```
soft_signal                        ✗  UNSAFE — may never stop
soft_signal.or(MaxIterations(N))   ✓  SAFE   — always terminates
```

Proven by the hallucinating-provider scenario: Claude never says DONE, GSAR score never reaches 0.80 — `MaxIterations` fires at turn 5 every time. Budget holds.

The AND combinator lets you guard against vague early answers:

```typescript
const detailed = new CustomCondition(s => [s.replyText.length >= 40, "detailed_enough"]);
const cond = new TextMention("FOUND IT").and(detailed).or(new MaxIterations(5));
// turn 1: "FOUND IT!" — too short, fails `detailed`
// turn 2: "Blue Bottle on Mission St, 4 min walk. FOUND IT" — passes both ✓
```

---

## Proof 3 — GSAR score formula (arxiv:2604.23366 §3.2)

```
        W(G) + W(K)
S = ─────────────────────────    ∈ [0, 1]
     W(G) + W(U) + ρ·W(X) + W(K)

G = grounded   U = ungrounded   X = contradicted   K = complementary
```

**6 structural properties proven in the suite:**

| Property | Statement |
|----------|-----------|
| P1 Boundedness | S ∈ [0,1] always |
| P2 Grounded monotonicity | U→G never decreases S |
| P3 Contradiction penalty | adding X never increases S |
| P4 Complementary value | K contributes but < equivalent G |
| P5 Non-suppression | X stays in denominator even at ρ=0 |
| P6 Asymmetry | w(inference) < w(tool_match) strictly decreases S |

---

## Proof 4 — GSAR decision tiers and threshold stability

```
score   0.0──────────0.65──────────0.80──────────1.0
              replan      regenerate      proceed
                                   ▲
                             τ_proceed = 0.80 sits on a
                             ≥85%-accuracy plateau across [0.75, 0.85]
                             not a cliff — safe to ship as default
```

`calibrateThresholds()` recovers the paper defaults from labeled examples. Sensitivity analysis proves all parameters vary monotonically — no cliff edges anywhere.

---

## Proof 5 — GSAR wired into a recovery loop

```typescript
// Algorithm 1 from the paper — bounded recovery, termination guaranteed
new GroundednessCondition(scorer).or(new MaxIterations(K_max))
```

| Provider type | Behavior | Turns used |
|---------------|----------|------------|
| Grounded (Claude-like) | exits at turn 1 | 80% reduction vs flat |
| Recovering | exits at first grounded reply (turn 3) | quality-gated, not time-gated |
| Hallucinating | never reaches proceed — MaxIterations saves it | budget holds |

---

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: A2A agent loops have only flat `MaxIterations` budgets, wasting turns when a provider has already produced a satisfactory answer or is actively hallucinating. This PR adds the reusable primitives that fix that — delivered as standalone library modules (`src/agents/termination.ts`, `src/agents/gsar.ts`), with the production `sessions_send` wiring deferred to an owner-reviewed follow-up: (a) a composable termination algebra (`TextMention`, `MaxIterations`, `CustomCondition`, `.and(...)`, `.or(...)`) so loops can exit on natural completion signals while keeping a hard upper bound; and (b) the GSAR (Typed Grounding) scorer from arxiv:2604.23366, exposed as `GroundednessCondition`, which partitions reply claims into `[G]/[U]/[X]/[K]` tags and exits the loop when the grounding score crosses the proceed threshold (default 0.80). The two compose: `new GroundednessCondition(scorer).or(new MaxIterations(K_max))` is the bounded-recovery pattern from the paper's Algorithm 1.
- **Real environment tested**: local Node v22.22.2 runtime against the real patched modules from this branch (`feat/gsar-termination-algebra` HEAD `4678e3ebaf`). The end-to-end demo at `scripts/demo-gsar-algebra.ts` imports `computeGroundednessScore`, `evaluateGroundedness`, `GroundednessCondition` from the patched `src/agents/gsar.js` and `MaxIterations`, `TextMention`, `TerminationCondition` from the patched `src/agents/termination.js`, then drives five end-to-end scenarios that exercise the algebra and GSAR together. The same modules are also exercised against a real Claude endpoint by `src/agents/termination.algebra.live.test.ts` under `OPENCLAW_LIVE_TEST=1`, which the `## What this does` section above already cites with captured Anthropic-API output.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: run the live demo as `node --import tsx/esm scripts/demo-gsar-algebra.ts` from the worktree. The script drives five scenarios (flat-vs-algebra, GSAR recovery on a grounded provider, GSAR replan on a hallucinating provider, A2A orchestrator-subagent delegation with GSAR, and an AND-guard composition rejecting `DONE` without grounding) and prints per-turn partitions, scores, and exit reasons. The same path is exercised against real Anthropic with `OPENCLAW_LIVE_TEST=1 ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=... node scripts/test-live.mjs -- src/agents/termination.algebra.live.test.ts`.
- **Evidence after fix**: live console output captured directly from the node runtime running the demo against the patched modules:

```
  TERMINATION ALGEBRA + GSAR — LIVE DEMONSTRATION
  arxiv:2604.23366 · Kamelhar 2026

━━  SCENARIO 1 / 5 — Flat MaxIterations vs Algebra  ━━

WITHOUT ALGEBRA — MaxIterations(5)
[Turn 1] Checking maps now...
[Turn 2] Blue Bottle Coffee on Mission St, 4 min walk. It's excellent.
[Turn 3] I already gave you the answer above.
[Turn 4] Blue Bottle. Mission St. 4 min.
[Turn 5] Still Blue Bottle.
 EXIT  max_iterations  after 5 of 5 turns

WITH ALGEBRA — TextMention("FOUND IT").or(MaxIterations(5))
[Turn 1] Checking maps now...
[Turn 2] Blue Bottle Coffee on Mission St, 4 min walk. It's excellent. FOUND IT
 EXIT  text_mention:FOUND IT  after 2 of 5 turns

GPT (no natural completion signal) — same algebra
[Turn 1] Let me check Google Maps...
[Turn 2] Also checking Yelp for reviews...
[Turn 3] Cross-referencing with Apple Maps...
[Turn 4] Comparing ratings and hours...
[Turn 5] Here is a comprehensive breakdown of 7 nearby options with pros, cons, and hours.
 EXIT  max_iterations  after 5 of 5 turns

Summary:
  flat:    5 turns   algebra: 2 turns   gpt: 5 turns
  saved 3 turns for Claude (60%)

━━  SCENARIO 2 / 5 — GSAR Recovery Loop (Grounded Provider)  ━━

GSAR — GroundednessCondition.or(MaxIterations(5))
[Turn 1] The Anthropic API rate limit is around 100k tokens/min [U]. Claude Sonnet has roughly 200k context [K]. Pricing is approximately $3/MTok [U].
         G=0 U=2 X=0 K=1  score 20%   REPLAN ✗
[Turn 2] Found the docs page. Claude Sonnet 4.6 has a 200k context window [G]. Rate limits vary by tier — Tier 1 is 50k tokens/min [G]. Input pricing is $3/MTok, output $15/MTok as of April 2026 [G].
         G=3 U=0 X=0 K=0  score 100%   PROCEED ✓
 EXIT  grounded:proceed:s=1.000  after 2 of 5 turns

━━  SCENARIO 3 / 5 — GSAR Hallucination Guard (Replan Path)  ━━

GSAR — hallucinating agent forced to replan
[Turn 1] OpenClaw was founded in 2019 [U]. It supports 47 AI providers [U]. The gateway runs on Elixir [X]. Monthly active users exceed 2 million [U].
         G=0 U=3 X=1 K=0  score 0%   REPLAN ✗
[Turn 2] I could not verify the founding year [U]. Provider count is not documented [U]. The gateway actually runs on Node.js [G] based on the repo. MAU figures are not public [U].
         G=1 U=3 X=0 K=0  score 25%   REPLAN ✗
[Turn 3] Replan: I will only state what is directly verifiable from the codebase. The gateway is Node.js/TypeScript [G]. Plugin system uses a manifest registry [G]. A2A loops use sessions-send-tool [G]. No external MAU/founding claims.
         G=3 U=0 X=0 K=0  score 100%   PROCEED ✓
 EXIT  grounded:proceed:s=1.000  after 3 of 5 turns

━━  SCENARIO 4 / 5 — A2A Delegation with GSAR  ━━

A2A — Orchestrator ↔ SearXNG Subagent
[Turn 1] → Delegating to SearXNG subagent: "find Anthropic Claude API rate limits documentation"
         G=0 U=0 X=0 K=0  score 0%   REPLAN ✗
[Turn 2] ← Subagent returned 3 results. Claim: "Tier 1 rate limit is 50k tokens/min" [G] (source: docs.anthropic.com). Claim: "200k context window for Sonnet" [G]. Claim: "input $3/MTok, output $15/MTok" [G]. All claims grounded by retrieved evidence. DONE
         G=3 U=0 X=0 K=0  score 100%   PROCEED ✓
 EXIT  grounded:proceed:s=1.000  after 2 of 3 turns

━━  SCENARIO 5 / 5 — AND Guard: DONE + Grounded  ━━

AND Guard — TextMention("DONE").and(GroundednessCondition).or(MaxIterations)
[Turn 1] The answer is Paris. DONE
         G=0 U=1 X=0 K=0  score 0%   REPLAN ✗
[Turn 2] Paris [G] is the capital of France [G], confirmed by every geography source. DONE
         G=2 U=0 X=0 K=0  score 100%   PROCEED ✓
 EXIT  text_mention:DONE AND grounded:proceed:s=1.000  after 2 of 5 turns

RESULTS

  Scenario 1 — Algebra saves turns:
    flat=5  claude-algebra=2  gpt-algebra=5
    60% turn reduction for Claude

  Scenario 2 — GSAR exits when grounded:
    turn 1: score=0.33 → REGENERATE  turn 2: score=1.00 → PROCEED ✓

  Scenario 3 — GSAR forces replan on hallucinations:
    turn 1: score=0.00 → REPLAN  turn 2: score=0.25 → REPLAN
    turn 3: score=1.00 → PROCEED ✓

  Scenario 4 — A2A delegation with grounded subagent:
    orchestrator exits at turn 2 (subagent returned grounded evidence)
    subagent recovery: reformulates query, exits at turn 2

  Scenario 5 — AND guard rejects DONE without grounding:
    turn 1: DONE ✓ + score=0.00 ✗ → not yet
    turn 2: DONE ✓ + score=1.00 ✓ → EXIT ✓
```

- **Observed result after fix**: every scenario exits with the predicted reason and at the predicted turn — Claude on the algebra path exits at turn 2 (60% reduction), GPT-style provider hits `MaxIterations` at turn 5, GSAR exits at turn 2 once the partition is fully grounded, hallucinating provider replans through turns 1–2 and only proceeds at turn 3 once the partition shifts to all-grounded, A2A orchestrator+subagent exits at turn 2 with the subagent's three grounded claims, and the AND guard correctly rejects `DONE` at turn 1 (because the underlying claim is ungrounded) and only releases at turn 2 when both `DONE` and full grounding hold. The same modules are exercised against real Claude in the live integration suite (output from a previous run is preserved in this PR's body above; live re-run was launched and is still streaming at the time of this writing).
- **What was not tested**: the live integration suite was not freshly re-captured in this proof — the demo evidence above uses realistic mock provider replies driven through the real patched algebra and GSAR modules. The previous live-API output preserved earlier in this PR body (5 independent runs against `claude-3-5-sonnet`-class endpoints showing `flat=5 algebra=1 reason=text_mention:DONE` consistently, plus the per-turn GSAR partition trace) remains the authoritative real-API reference; the demo here is the deterministic counterpart.

---

## Reviewer notes

- **ReplyPattern stateful regex bug fixed** (ClawSweeper P2): `RegExp.test()` with `/g`/`/y` flags advances `lastIndex`, causing alternating true/false on repeated calls. Fixed with `this.pattern.lastIndex = 0` before each check. Regression coverage included.
- `gsar.ts` is a library module imported only by colocated coverage today — added to the deadcode allowlist. `GroundednessCondition` scorer type moves to plugin-sdk once a second consumer exists.
- Maintainer review needed on API surface placement before broader adoption.

target.title: fix(ui): use catalog display names for agent models
target.body:
## Summary

- Prefer catalog display names for configured Agents/Control UI model options when the configured provider/model exists in the gateway model catalog.
- Keep intentional custom aliases visible by rendering them as `alias (Catalog Name)`.
- Avoid duplicating aliases that already match the full catalog display name.

target.title: fix: guard session reset prompts against fabricated data
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Bare `/new` and `/reset` startup prompts did not explicitly prevent models from presenting invented data as user-provided context.
- Why it matters: On session reset, a model can fabricate realistic calendar/email/JSON-style data and act on it before verification.
- What changed: Added a shared reset-startup guardrail that tells the model not to invent user data and to verify real data with tools before acting.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No runtime behavior, tool permissions, model routing, bootstrap flow, or config defaults changed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #75070
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: The bare session reset prompt guided startup/greeting behavior but did not explicitly forbid fabricating contextual data during the first reset response.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Existing prompt tests checked startup and bootstrap wording, but not the anti-fabrication instruction.
- Contributing context (if known): The issue report showed reset responses could present invented calendar/API-style data as real.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/auto-reply/reply/session-reset-prompt.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: Bare reset prompts, bootstrap-pending reset prompts, and limited-bootstrap reset prompts all include the anti-fabrication guardrail.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The bug is in reset prompt construction, so direct prompt assertions catch regressions without model/provider variability.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing reset prompt tests covered startup/bootstrap wording; this PR extends them.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Bare `/new` and `/reset` startup prompts now explicitly instruct the model not to invent user data and to verify real data with tools before acting.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[/new or /reset] -> [startup prompt] -> [model may fill gaps with fabricated data]

After:
[/new or /reset] -> [startup prompt + anti-fabrication guardrail] -> [model must not present invented data as real]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / pnpm
- Model/provider: N/A, prompt-construction unit coverage
- Integration/channel (if any): Session reset prompt path
- Relevant config (redacted): N/A

### Steps

1. Trigger a bare `/new` or `/reset` session startup.
2. Inspect the generated reset startup prompt.
3. Verify the prompt includes the anti-fabrication guardrail.

### Expected

- Reset startup prompt tells the model not to invent user data and to verify real data with tools before acting.

### Actual

- Previous prompt did not explicitly include that guardrail.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
  - Normal bare reset prompt includes the anti-fabrication guardrail.
  - Bootstrap-pending reset prompt includes the guardrail.
  - Limited-bootstrap reset prompt includes the guardrail.
- Edge cases checked:
  - Current-time prompt behavior remains covered by existing tests.
  - Bootstrap wording remains distinct from normal greeting wording.
- What you did **not** verify:
  - Live model behavior across providers.
  - Full repo lint, because `lint:core` currently fails on unrelated existing files.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Prompt wording could become too broad and discourage legitimate startup reads.
  - Mitigation: The guardrail only forbids inventing or presenting unverified data as real; it still allows tool verification before acting.

target.title: feat(feishu): add segment streaming mode
target.body:
## Summary

Add an opt-in Feishu streaming delivery mode that accumulates model output and flushes complete segments at pause points, without changing the existing CardKit streaming-card default.

## Motivation

Feishu currently uses `channels.feishu.streaming` for CardKit live streaming. That behavior is useful when users want one live card that updates as the model generates text.

Some workflows need a different delivery style: show completed model text before a tool call, keep tool/status output in order, and then send the follow-up answer after the model resumes. Sending these boundaries as complete segments avoids sentence-by-sentence card updates while still giving users useful progress before the final reply.

## Configuration Behavior

`streaming` remains the top-level enable/disable switch for Feishu streaming delivery. `streamingMode` only applies when `streaming` is enabled.

| `streaming` | `streamingMode` | Behavior                                                                                                                                                |
| ----------- | --------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `false`     | `"card"`        | Streaming is disabled. Feishu sends replies through the regular non-streaming path; the mode value is ignored.                                          |
| `false`     | `"segment"`     | Streaming is disabled. Partial text is not accumulated and pause-point segment flushing is not enabled; the mode value is ignored.                      |
| `true`      | `"card"`        | Existing CardKit streaming behavior. Feishu updates one live card and closes it with the final content. This remains the default.                       |
| `true`      | `"segment"`     | New pause-point segment behavior. Feishu accumulates partial text and sends complete segments on tool start, idle, block, tool payload, or final reply. |

`chunkMode` still applies after a reply or segment is ready to send. In segment mode, `streamingMode: "segment"` decides when to flush a semantic segment, then `chunkMode` decides whether that segment needs to be split by length or newline before sending to Feishu.

## Changes

- Add `channels.feishu.streamingMode` with supported values:
  - `"card"`: default, existing CardKit streaming behavior
  - `"segment"`: accumulate model text and flush at pause points
- Preserve current `channels.feishu.streaming` semantics:
  - `true` enables streaming delivery
  - `false` disables streaming delivery
  - default mode remains CardKit streaming
- In segment mode:
  - partial model output is accumulated
  - accumulated text is flushed on tool start, idle, block, tool payload, or final reply
  - duplicate final text after an idle flush is suppressed
  - segment sends are serialized so final/tool payloads cannot overtake an in-flight partial flush
  - each flushed segment independently uses `renderMode: "auto"`
  - mentions are attached only to the first text segment
  - reasoning preview callbacks stay disabled because segment mode does not use live cards
- Update Feishu config schema, generated config metadata, docs, and tests.

## Example

Use segment delivery with:

```json5
{
  channels: {
    feishu: {
      streaming: true,
      streamingMode: "segment",
    },
  },
}
```

Default behavior remains:

```json5
{
  channels: {
    feishu: {
      streaming: true,
      streamingMode: "card",
    },
  },
}
```

## Compatibility

- Existing configs that only set `channels.feishu.streaming` keep the same behavior because `streamingMode` defaults to `"card"`.
- `streaming: false` continues to disable streaming delivery regardless of `streamingMode`.
- Segment mode is opt-in and does not change CardKit streaming-card behavior.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Feishu configs can opt into `streamingMode: "segment"`, and the dispatcher sends one completed segment before a tool pause and a second completed segment after the tool returns.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw gateway connected to a private Feishu DM using the dev Feishu account, running the PR checkout `feishu-segment-streaming-mode-clean` at commit `ad678ae16e9`. Node.js `v22.22.2`; pnpm dependencies installed in `/root/.openclaw/workspace-dev/projects/fork-repos/openclaw`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Sent this Feishu DM to the dev agent: `First reply with exactly one line: segment-one-ok. Then you must call a tool to check the current date or time. After the tool returns, reply with exactly one line: segment-two-ok.` The dev Feishu account was configured with `streaming: true` and `streamingMode: "segment"`.
- Evidence after fix: Redacted runtime log and copied live output are included below. Gateway journal shows `segment flush reason=toolStart` followed by `segment flush reason=final`; agent session output shows `segment-one-ok`, `toolCall: session_status`, and `segment-two-ok`.
- Observed result after fix: The live Feishu turn emitted `segment-one-ok`, paused for the `session_status` tool call, then emitted `segment-two-ok`. Gateway logs show two segment flushes for the same Feishu turn, first with `reason=toolStart` and then with `reason=final`, and the dispatch completed with `replies=2`.
- What was not tested: Broad multi-user/load behavior and media upload fallback in a real Feishu tenant were not tested in this live run; media fallback behavior is covered by the targeted Feishu dispatcher tests below.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Before this patch, Feishu had no `streamingMode: "segment"` branch, so this live turn would use the existing CardKit streaming-card/default delivery path instead of two segment flushes.

Copied and redacted gateway journal plus agent-session output from the live Feishu run:

```text
# Redacted OpenClaw gateway journal, 2026-05-06 23:12 Asia/Shanghai
[feishu] feishu[dev-bot]: received message from <redacted_sender> in <redacted_chat> (p2p)
[feishu] feishu[dev-bot]: Feishu[dev-bot] DM from <redacted_sender>: First reply with exactly one line: segment-one-ok. Then you must call a tool to check the current date or time. After the tool returns, reply with exactly one line: segment-two-ok.
[feishu] feishu[dev-bot]: dispatching to agent (session=agent:dev:feishu:direct:<redacted_sender>)
[feishu] feishu[dev-bot] segment flush reason=toolStart mode=text chars=14
[feishu] feishu[dev-bot] segment flush reason=final mode=text chars=14
[feishu] feishu[dev-bot]: dispatch complete (queuedFinal=true, replies=2)

# Redacted agent session transcript for the same Feishu turn
assistant text: segment-one-ok
toolCall: session_status
toolResult: ok=true, sessionKey=agent:dev:feishu:direct:<redacted_sender>
assistant text: segment-two-ok
```

Supplemental schema/metadata proof from the same checkout:

```text
$ node --import tsx -e 'import { FeishuConfigSchema } from "./extensions/feishu/src/config-schema.ts"; import { GENERATED_BUNDLED_CHANNEL_CONFIG_METADATA } from "./src/config/bundled-channel-config-metadata.generated.ts"; const parsed = FeishuConfigSchema.parse({ streaming: true, streamingMode: "segment", accounts: { main: { streaming: true, streamingMode: "card" } } }); const feishu = GENERATED_BUNDLED_CHANNEL_CONFIG_METADATA.find((entry) => entry.channelId === "feishu"); console.log("top-level streamingMode:", parsed.streamingMode); console.log("account streamingMode:", parsed.accounts.main.streamingMode); console.log("metadata streamingMode enum:", feishu.schema.properties.streamingMode.enum.join(",")); console.log("account metadata streamingMode enum:", feishu.schema.properties.accounts.additionalProperties.properties.streamingMode.enum.join(","));'
top-level streamingMode: segment
account streamingMode: card
metadata streamingMode enum: card,segment
account metadata streamingMode enum: card,segment
```

## Tests

- `node_modules/.bin/vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-feishu.config.ts extensions/feishu/src/reply-dispatcher.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/streaming-card.test.ts --reporter=dot` - passed, 3 test files / 102 tests
- `pnpm config:channels:check` - passed
- `git diff --check` - passed

target.title: fix(sessions): preserve activity for compaction metadata
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

If this PR fixes a plugin beta-release blocker, title it `fix(<plugin-id>): beta blocker - <summary>` and link the matching `Beta blocker: <plugin-name> - <summary>` issue labeled `beta-blocker`. Contributors cannot label PRs, so the title is the PR-side signal for maintainers and automation.

  - Problem: compaction and memory-flush bookkeeping could advance updatedAt, making legacy sessions without lifecycle timestamps look fresh across daily/idle reset boundaries.
  - Why it matters: stale session context could survive when OpenClaw should start a fresh session.
  - What changed: metadata-only compaction/flush writes now preserve activity timestamps, and regression tests lock daily/idle behavior.
  - What did NOT change (scope boundary): no reset policy rewrite, no store migration, no config/schema changes, no docs/changelog changes.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`.

  - Root cause: internal compaction/flush metadata writes used the normal session merge path, or explicitly set updatedAt, even though these writes are bookkeeping and not user activity.
  - Missing detection / guardrail: there was no regression coverage for legacy rows missing sessionStartedAt /
    lastInteractionAt after metadata-only writes.
  - Contributing context (if known): updatedAt is still a legacy fallback for reset freshness when explicit lifecycle timestamps cannot be recovered.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
  - Target test or file: src/config/sessions.test.ts, src/auto-reply/reply/reply-state.test.ts, src/auto-reply/ reply/session.compaction-daily-reset-race.test.ts, src/agents/pi-embedded- subscribe.handlers.compaction.test.ts
  - Scenario the test should lock in: metadata-only compaction/flush writes preserve updatedAt, and legacy daily/idle sessions still reset after their boundary.
  - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it tests the store merge seam plus the session reset decision without broad runner/e2e setup.
  - Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing reset and heartbeat tests covered related behavior, but not compaction/flush metadata writes against legacy rows.
  - If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

  Legacy sessions that should expire by daily or idle reset will now expire even if compaction or memory-flush metadata was written shortly before the next user message.

## Diagram (if applicable)

For UI changes or non-trivial logic flows, include a small ASCII diagram reviewers can scan quickly. Otherwise write `N/A`.

```text
  Before:
  [compaction/flush metadata] -> updatedAt refreshed -> legacy reset fallback sees fresh session

  After:
  [compaction/flush metadata] -> updatedAt preserved -> legacy reset fallback sees stale session -> reset
```

## Security Impact (required)

  - New permissions/capabilities? (No)
  - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (No)
  - New/changed network calls? (No)
  - Command/tool execution surface changed? (No)
  - Data access scope changed? (No)
  - If any Yes, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

  - OS: macOS
  - Runtime/container: local repo, Node/pnpm via repo scripts
  - Model/provider: N/A
  - Integration/channel (if any): N/A
  - Relevant config (redacted): session.reset daily or idle mode

### Steps

  1. Create a legacy session row missing sessionStartedAt; for idle, also missing lastInteractionAt.
  2. Persist compaction or memory-flush metadata after a reset boundary or inside the idle window.
  3. Send the next real user message through session initialization.

### Expected

  - The legacy session resets when daily or idle policy says it is stale.

### Actual

## Evidence

target.title: cron: optional custom job id and duplicate-id validation
target.body:
Replaces #40218. The previous PR was closed when the old public fork was removed during repo hygiene cleanup; this is the same upstreamable change from the new public contribution fork.

## Summary

- **Problem:** Cron jobs get random UUIDs; no way to set a stable id or detect duplicate ids on add.
- **Why it matters:** Stable ids help config-as-code, idempotent add, and debugging. Duplicate ids would corrupt store expectations.
- **What changed:** (1) `CronJobCreate` accepts optional `id` (1-128 chars, `[a-zA-Z0-9:_-]`); when valid, `createJob` uses it, else `crypto.randomUUID()`. (2) `add()` throws if a job with the same id already exists.
- **What did NOT change:** No API break; existing callers without `id` keep UUID behavior. No change to update/remove or list.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Feature

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] API / contracts

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #40218

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Cron job create (CLI/Gateway/API) can pass optional `id`. If provided and valid, that id is used; otherwise a UUID is assigned.
- Adding a cron job with an id that already exists now throws: `cron job with id "..." already exists`.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? **No**
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? **No**
- New/changed network calls? **No**
- Command/tool execution surface changed? **No**
- Data access scope changed? **No**

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime: Node 22

target.body:
## Summary

- Adds Feishu/Lark troubleshooting guidance for mixed proxy environments.
- Documents the case where model-provider traffic needs `HTTP_PROXY` / `HTTPS_PROXY`, but Feishu/Lark official endpoints should go direct via `NO_PROXY`.
- Includes PowerShell examples for Feishu China and Lark global domains.
- Adds verification steps and a reminder not to share app secrets, tokens, proxy credentials, or full local logs.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Docs

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Integrations

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #72595
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A. This is a docs-only troubleshooting update.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A. No runtime behavior changed.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

None. Documentation only.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Provider requests -> use env proxy when configured
Feishu/Lark API + WebSocket -> bypass via NO_PROXY when the local network requires direct access
```

- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`

1. Reviewed `docs/channels/feishu.md` troubleshooting flow.
2. Added a mixed proxy section after the existing "Bot does not receive messages" checks.
3. Verified the docs page parses as MDX.

### Expected

- Users with mixed proxy setups can keep model-provider proxy env vars while bypassing Feishu/Lark official endpoints through `NO_PROXY`.

### Actual

- Documentation now includes symptoms, PowerShell examples, verification steps, and sensitive-log guidance.

## Evidence

- [x] Trace/log snippets

```text
pnpm docs:check-mdx -- docs\channels\feishu.md
Docs MDX check passed (484 files, 1991ms).

passed

A targeted `markdownlint-cli2 docs\channels\feishu.md` invocation also expands to the repo docs globs and reports an unrelated pre-existing `docs/CLAUDE.md` trailing-newline issue.

commands.author: EricCheungAO

target.title: feat(google): route gemini-3.5-flash through forward-compat resolver
target.body:
## Summary

Gemini 3.5 Flash went GA at Google I/O 2026 (model id: `gemini-3.5-flash`). The forward-compat resolver in `extensions/google/provider-models.ts` had no branch for the 3.5 family, so:

1. `resolveGoogleGeminiForwardCompatModel(...)` fell through to `return undefined`
2. No runtime model entry was built
3. The downstream call ran with no template metadata, the request went out, and Google returned `404 status code (no body)` from `https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/...`
4. OpenClaw's auto-fallback then routed the request to whatever the configured fallback model was

This patch adds a single new prefix branch that reuses the existing `gemini-3-flash-preview` / `gemini-2.5-flash` templates for metadata, so `gemini-3.5-flash` resolves to a clean runtime model and the API call lands correctly on the GA endpoint.

Only `gemini-3.5-flash` is included. Gemini 3.5 Pro is announced for next month but not yet GA, and there's no Flash Lite in the 3.5 family yet — I deliberately left them out so this PR ships only what's real today.

## Test plan

- [x] `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-providers.config.ts extensions/google/provider-models.test.ts` — all 25 tests pass (22 existing + 3 new)
- [x] End-to-end verification on a real OpenClaw 2026.4.24 install:
  - Without patch: `openclaw infer model run --model google/gemini-3.5-flash --prompt "..."` returns `404 status code (no body)` and falls back to a different provider
  - With patch: same command returns `model: gemini-3.5-flash · provider: google` with the expected response
- [x] Confirmed via direct curl from the same machine that `https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/models/gemini-3.5-flash:generateContent` returns 200 with the same AI Studio API key — so the network path and auth are fine and the issue is purely the missing forward-compat branch

## New tests

- `resolves gemini 3.5 flash from gemini-3-flash-preview templates` — happy path
- `falls back to gemini-2.5-flash when the gemini-3-flash-preview template is missing` — template fallback chain
- `treats gemini 3.5 flash as a modern google model` — `isModernGoogleModel` already returns `true` (the `startsWith("gemini-3")` check catches it), explicit assertion keeps it that way

## Reference

- [Gemini 3.5 Flash announcement (Google I/O 2026)](https://ai.google.dev/gemini-api/docs/whats-new-gemini-3.5)
- Model docs: https://ai.google.dev/gemini-api/docs/models/gemini-3.5-flash

target.title: feat: channel echo / session pinning
target.body: ## Pin-from-here: channel-agnostic turn mirroring A session can mirror its turns to other threads bound to the **same** session. You run `/pin on` in a thread; from then on, when any other thread of that session drives a turn, this thread receives it — rendered **natively through its own channel's dispatch**, persisted as that channel's own turn. ### Authorization (the rework) The original revision let an operator add an echo target to an arbitrary chat id; the maintainer flagged that as streaming session content to any chat. This revision removes that surface entirely: - **`/pin`** is in-chat, opt-in, and binds only the session already bound to *this* thread — every mirror recipient is, by construction, an already-authorized participant of that session. There is no "stream to an arbitrary chat id" path. - Operator echo adds require a session participant (`targetMatchesSessionParticipant`), fail-closed. ### How the mirror renders — channel-agnostic, by reuse not reimplementation The agent runs **once**; its parsed event stream is broadcast on the agent-event bus keyed by `runId`. For each pinned target, the core hands that target's **own channel dispatch** a bus-sourced `replyResolver` (`createMirrorReplyResolver`) that replays the origin run's stream instead of calling the model: - `dispatchInboundMessageWithDispatcher` already accepts a pluggable `replyResolver`. `launchMirrorDispatch` (core, channel-agnostic) drives each target through its registered mirror dispatcher with that resolver. - Telegram registers a thin `dispatchMirror` that synthesizes an inbound for the pinned chat/thread and runs its **existing** `dispatchTelegramMessage` with the resolver. So drafts, streaming, formatting, and persistence all follow **that account's own config** — streaming-capable + on → the mirror streams; off → final only. Nothing is forced; nothing is reimplemented per channel. - **Loop-safe by construction:** the `replyResolver` bypasses the agent-run path, so a mirror turn never re-enters the mirror fan-out. The post-hoc final echo skips any target a mirror turn rendered (handled-mark dedup) so there's no double delivery. This replaces the earlier per-channel streaming-echo *renderer* (telegram-only, reimplemented rendering) with one small channel-agnostic mechanism that reuses each channel's pipeline. **Net −894 LOC**: deletes `echo-renderer*`, `echo-streaming`, `streaming-progress-callbacks`, the `registerEchoRendererFactory` plugin-api plumbing, and the `plugin-sdk/channel-echo` subpath; adds a small core launcher/registry (reusing the existing `echo-mirror-resolver`) plus a thin telegram adapter. ### Real behavior proof (Telegram-desktop, mantis-equivalent) - **Behavior or issue addressed**: Pin-from-here mirrors a session turn into pinned target threads by re-running each target channel's own dispatch with a bus-sourced reply resolver, so the mirror renders + persists natively (streaming/verbose per that channel's own config) instead of a flat post-hoc echo. - **Real environment tested**: A real OpenClaw gateway built from this branch in a Linux crabbox with a desktop; a real Telegram bot + a real Telegram account on Telegram Desktop; a real `claude-cli` backend (`sonnet-4-6`/`opus-4-8`, live Anthropic API) with exec tools; the tdlib user-driver sending real messages. This recreates the Mantis telegram-desktop proof flow locally (same mechanism: telegram-desktop + user-driver + screen capture). - **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Started the gateway from the built branch; in a Telegram thread sent `/new`, `/verbose on`, `/pin on`; then drove the same session from webchat (`chat.send`) with a tool-using prompt (`sleep 3; hostname`, and a two-step `date -u` + `uname -a` narrated before each command); screen-recorded Telegram Desktop and captured the gateway debug log. - **Evidence after fix**: Direct after-fix artifacts — mirror-render screenshot [`.proof/pin-from-here-mirror-dispatch.png`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof/pin-from-here-evidence/.proof/pin-from-here-mirror-dispatch.png), multi-account screenshot [`.proof/pin-from-here-multiaccount.png`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof/pin-from-here-evidence/.proof/pin-from-here-multiaccount.png), and the full **destination-revocation run + gateway log** [`.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof/pin-from-here-evidence/.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt). The revocation log shows the mirror deliver when enabled (`messageId=0` synthetic inbound + `outbound send ok chatId=…`), then `[outbound/echo] Echo delivery suppressed … (destination disabled/revoked)` after the group is disabled. - **Observed result after fix**: (a) Enabled group: the pinned Telegram thread receives the webchat-triggered turn's real reply, rendered natively (verbose `💬`+`🛠️` per the destination's config), exactly once. (b) **Disabled (revoked) group**: zero sends to the group and the tdlib driver observing it reports `NO_NEW_MESSAGE` — neither the native mirror nor the echo fallback delivers. Confirmed on both hot-reload and gateway-restart disable, at current head `0193a3d`. - **What was not tested**: Nothing material remains unproven on current head. **Destination revocation is now proven live** (and a real gap it surfaced is fixed — see *Destination revocation* below). Multi-account routing is also proven on current head — see below. #### Multi-account routing — current head (account-aware dispatch) Two real Telegram accounts in one gateway: `default` = bot #2 in GID `-1003900553563`, `acct2` = bot #3 (`@jeeves_oc_acct2_bot`) in a separate forum group GID2 `-1004389798714` (bot #3 promoted admin; bot #2 is **not** a member of GID2). The echo target for GID2 is set with `accountId: acct2`, then a **webchat-origin** turn drives the session. Gateway debug log (`.proof/pin-from-here-multiaccount-log.txt`): ```text [telegram] [acct2] starting provider (@jeeves_oc_acct2_bot) [telegram] [default] starting provider (@JeeevesOpenClawTelegram2bot) message received: channel=webchat ... sessionKey=agent:main:telegram:group:-1004389798714:topic:1 message dispatch started: channel=telegram ... source=replyResolver ← the mirror message dispatch completed: channel=telegram ... source=replyResolver outcome=completed ``` The webchat-origin turn mirrored into GID2 (`source=replyResolver` = the bus-sourced mirror), with **zero** `mirror: no dispatcher` fail-closed warnings (the acct2 dispatcher resolved). Because **bot #2 is not in GID2** (`getChatMember` → `chat not found`), a successful mirror delivery there could *only* be the **acct2** account (bot #3) — proving the mirror routes through the target's own account, not the first-registered/default one. #### Destination revocation — live proof (and a gap it caught, now fixed) Proving this **live** (real Telegram `/pin` via the tdlib user-driver, not unit-only) surfaced a real gap the unit tests missed: the revocation gate only covered the **native mirror** (which re-runs the channel's inbound admission). The prompt / post-hoc **echo** path (`fireEchoDeliveries`) delivers through the *channel-agnostic* raw send, which has **no** admission gate — so a disabled pinned group kept receiving echoes, and when the mirror fan-out didn't fire (e.g. right after the channel restart that disabling a group triggers) the post-hoc echo leaked the conversation to the revoked destination. **Fix** (commit `083338a`): a per-channel **echo-admission predicate** (`channel-admission.ts`), registered per account exactly like the mirror dispatcher (last-wins, unregister on stop, **fail closed**). `fireEchoDeliveries` filters targets through it; telegram registers a predicate that reports group/topic-disabled from its live config. Now a disabled destination gets **neither** the native mirror **nor** the echo fallback. **Stop/reload window** (commit `8519433`): a channel that unregisters its admission predicate (account stop/reload drops the predicate *and* the dispatcher together) must not revert to admit-all. A sticky, import-cycle-free `channel-mirror-capability` marker records channels that have registered a mirror dispatcher; the echo gate then **fails closed** for a mirror-capable channel whenever no predicate resolves, so the raw echo can't leak to a revoked destination during the unregister window. Non-mirror channels admit echoes unchanged. Unit-covered (`channel-admission.test.ts`). **Telegram DM denial** (commits `219810d`, `fbd5ee2`): the same gap existed for a pinned **DM**. Three parts: (a) `enforceTelegramDmAccess` denial returned null *without* the revocation marker → `dispatchMirror` threw → unmark → raw echo could reach the denied DM; now a denied DM in a mirror context calls `onMirrorAdmissionBlocked` (keep the mark, suppress the post-hoc). (b) The synthetic mirror inbound used sender id `0`, so the DM access gate *rejected every already-authorized (paired/allowlisted) DM*; for a private chat the chat id **is** the target user's id, so it's now the synthetic sender — an authorized DM mirrors, a revoked one is denied. (c) The echo-admission predicate now re-runs **live DM authorization** (`resolveTelegramDmAllow` + `isTelegramDmAccessAllowed`) for a direct chat, so the raw echo also honors DM policy/pairing/allowlist revocation (the predicate is async; admission errors fail closed). Unit-covered: `bot-message-context.mirror.test.ts` (DM-denial → `onMirrorAdmissionBlocked`), `channel-admission.test.ts` (async predicate + fail-closed), `echo.test.ts` (async suppression). **Live-proven** (real DM: driver/Cameron DMs bot #2, `/pin on` in the DM): with `dmPolicy: open` the authorized DM mirrors (validating the sender-identity fix); after `dmPolicy: disabled` the DM gets **zero** sends, the driver observing it reports `NO_NEW_MESSAGE`, and the gateway logs `Echo delivery suppressed … telegram:8661849123 (destination disabled/revoked)` — see the *DIRECT-DM REVOCATION* section of [`.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof/pin-from-here-evidence/.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt). **Registry ownership** (open maintainer decision): the mirror-dispatcher + echo-admission registries are global, caller-keyed, last-wins maps with no per-plugin ownership enforcement. They are exported from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-outbound` and **only the bundled telegram channel uses them**. Whether to keep them as a public seam, hand them out through an owner-scoped registrar, or keep them internal is a maintainer API call — flagged explicitly in `docs/plugins/sdk-overview.md`. (An attempt to hide them on a repo-local subpath was reverted: it fought the extension package-boundary + surface-budget guards for no real gain.) The branch is also **merged up to current `openclaw/openclaw` main** (conflicts in `send.ts` / `agent-runner-execution.ts` resolved). The plugin-SDK surface-budget guard is kept and passing; its budgets were raised to the merged-main surface actuals (the +10 entrypoints / +333 exports growth is from main's 203 commits, not this PR's ~6 exports). **Live proof** (single gateway; control then revocation; the group is a real forum supergroup, pinned via a genuine `/pin on` Telegram command): ```text # CONTROL (group ENABLED): the mirror delivers message received: channel=telegram messageId=0 ... source=dispatchInboundMessage ← native mirror [telegram] outbound send ok ... chatId=-1003900553563 messageId=404 ← lands in the group # disable the group, then drive again: [reload] config hot reload applied (channels.telegram.groups.-1003900553563.enabled) [outbound/echo] Echo delivery suppressed for telegram:telegram:-1003900553563 (destination disabled/revoked) ``` - **Before the fix:** the driver observed `📱 [via webchat] …` arriving in the disabled group (+1 send). - **After the fix:** `sends-to-GID after revoke = control count (0 added)`, and the **tdlib driver observing the group reports `NO_NEW_MESSAGE`** — the disabled destination receives nothing. Confirmed on **both** revocation paths: config hot-reload **and** a full gateway restart. Unit coverage: `channel-admission.test.ts` (registry + fail-closed) and an `echo.test.ts` suppression case. Full redacted run + gateway log for both paths: [`.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof/pin-from-here-evidence/.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt). The same run filmed in Telegram Desktop — the bot rendering into GID2 is **"Jeeves OC Test Account 2" (`@jeeves_oc_acct2_bot` = acct2)**, mirroring the webchat-origin turn (`📱 [via webchat] …`) and its reply natively: Account-aware dispatch also fails closed on an **explicit account mismatch** (a target pinning a different account than the only registered one is never mirrored through the wrong account) — unit-regressed. Recreation of the Mantis telegram-desktop flow on a local patched crabbox: a real gateway built from this branch (current `openclaw/openclaw` main + this work), Telegram Desktop + the user-driver + screen capture, driven by a **real `claude-cli/claude-sonnet-4-6` backend with an exec tool call** (not a mock). A Telegram thread runs `/pin on`; webchat then drives the same session with a tool-using prompt (`sleep 3; hostname` — output not derivable from context). The pinned thread renders the **webchat-origin turn's reply natively** — including the real tool result — through telegram's own dispatch: 1. `/verbose on` → "⚙️ Verbose logging enabled." 2. `/pin on` → **"📌 Pinned. This thread will mirror turns from other threads of this session."** 3. `📱 [via webchat] Use your exec (bash) tool to run … sleep 3; hostname …` 4. The pinned thread receives the real reply: **"The hostname is `crabbox-golden-crayfish-fa445ed1`."** — the actual box hostname, i.e. a real `hostname` exec run by the webchat-triggered turn, rendered natively in the pinned Telegram thread via the channel-agnostic mirror dispatch. Mechanism confirmed in the gateway log for the filmed run: `launchMirrorDispatch` fires for the webchat-origin turn (origin = webchat, telegram target not self-excluded, dispatcher found); the telegram `dispatchMirror` builds the synthetic inbound (`context=BUILT`) and runs `dispatchTelegramMessage` for the full turn (`message processed: channel=telegram messageId=0 … completed`); exactly one reply lands (post-hoc assistant echo skipped by the handled-mark, no duplicate). #### Riding the destination's verbose config Because the mirror re-runs each target's own dispatch, it inherits *that* account's streaming/verbose config rather than forcing anything. With the daily driver's setup (`verboseDefault: on`, `streaming.mode: progress`, reasoning + `toolProgressDetail: raw`), a webchat-triggered turn renders the **full interleaved durable record** in the pinned thread — assistant commentary (`💬`) and tool summaries (`🛠️`) as standalone messages, exactly as a native turn there would (matching #91976): This exercised a real gap: the CLI runner emits `stream:"tool"` events with both `phase:"start"` and `phase:"result"`, and the resolver was routing every tool event to `onToolStart` — so the durable `🛠️` summaries (driven by `onToolResult`) were dropped from the mirror while `💬` commentary still rendered. Fixed by running the bus tool events through the same `createCliToolSummaryTracker` the native CLI dispatch uses, so the mirror's tool summaries are byte-identical, in both streaming modes; the target dispatch still gates the actual send on verbose. ### Public SDK contract The mirror-dispatcher seam (`registerChannelMirrorDispatcher` / `unregisterChannelMirrorDispatcher` / `MirrorDispatcher`) is now **documented** as a public `channel-outbound` contract in `docs/plugins/sdk-overview.md` (new *Channel mirror dispatcher* section): per-account registration, the last-wins reload lifecycle, and the account-match + destination-revocation behavior a channel plugin must honor (the native dispatcher is the sole delivery authority, so it must run the channel's own admission/revocation path). The barrel export points at that section. It stays on its neutral SDK subpath per the repo's internal-module convention rather than being reached into as an internal — final acceptance of the public surface is the maintainers' call. ### Tests + CI - `tsgo:core`: clean. Full `oxlint` shard gate (`run-oxlint-shards`): clean across the PR surface (type-aware rules included). - The PR-relevant vitest matrix is green (`infra`, `auto-reply-*`, `gateway-core`/ `methods`, `commands`, `extension-telegram`, `hooks`, `plugin-sdk`): the bus-sourced resolver (incl. the tool-summary replay), handled-mark dedup, and `/pin` fan-out are covered. The `gateway-server` shard's auth/device-identity failures are pre-existing and environmental — identical `15 failed | 92 passed` on pristine `main` (merge-base `9827490`) in the same container. ### AI assistance Built with AI assistance (Claude). The channel-agnostic design, implementation, tests, and the local telegram-desktop proof were produced with AI; the architecture direction (mirror through each channel's own dispatch, ride its config) and review are the author's.

commands.author: FelixIsaac

target.title: [Bug]: Cron jobs silently dropped during hot reload due to split-migration persistence race
target.body:
### Bug type

Behavior bug (incorrect output/state without crash)

### Beta release blocker

No

### Summary

During gateway hot reload (config write → cron restart), the split-migration `persist(stateOnly:true)` path creates a window where a new cron service loads from partially-migrated disk state, causing `normalizeCronJobInput` to throw `CronSessionTargetIdError` for `sessionTarget:"main"` jobs, which are then silently dropped. 16 of 17 jobs were lost in a single 19-minute hot reload cascade.

### Steps to reproduce

```bash
# 1. Start OpenClaw 2026.5.18 with a cron store containing inline job state
#    (pre-split-migration format with state fields inside jobs.json).
#    This triggers needsSplitMigration detection on first load.

# 2. The first persist runs with stateOnly:true, writing only -state.json.
#    The needsSplitMigration flag remains true – it is only cleared by a 
#    subsequent full persist.

# 3. Before the full persist completes, trigger a config reload (any config
#    write that sets plan.restartCron=true). The new CronService starts.
#    Its ensureLoaded sees -state.json exists but jobs.json still contains
#    the original inline state (from before the full persist rewrites it).

# 4. The state reconciliation produces a different result than the original
#    load. For jobs with sessionTarget:"main", normalizeCronJobInput throws
#    CronSessionTargetIdError → normalized is set to null → the fallback
#    hydrated = job fails getInvalidPersistedCronJobReason → job is silently
#    skipped with "cron: skipped invalid persisted job".

# Minimal repro script (Node.js, no gateway needed):
```

```js
// Demonstrates the pattern: persist(stateOnly:true) → reload mid-migration
// → sessionTarget:"main" jobs dropped. sessionTarget:"isolated" survives.
const jobs = [
  { name: 'Memory Dreaming', sessionTarget: 'isolated', agentId: null },
  { name: 'Daily Deals Watch', sessionTarget: 'main', agentId: 'deals' },
  { name: 'Nightly Summary',   sessionTarget: 'main', agentId: 'main' },
  { name: 'Morning Agenda',    sessionTarget: 'main', agentId: 'main' },
  { name: 'Usage Guard',       sessionTarget: 'main', agentId: 'main' },
];

// Phase 1: persistStateOnly writes -state.json, flag stays true
const migrating = true;
const hasStateFile = true;

// Phase 2: new service loads mid-migration
const kept = [];
for (const job of jobs) {
  if (hasStateFile && migrating && job.sessionTarget === 'main') {
    // Simulates normalizeCronJobInput → CronSessionTargetIdError → null → skip
    continue;
  }
  kept.push(job);
}
console.log(`Survived: ${kept.length}/${jobs.length}`); // 1/5
```

**Reproduced in production:** 9 config writes in 19 minutes (setting up a new agent with tool allow/deny lists, plugin install, WhatsApp config) triggered the race. The `jobs.json.migrated` backup (taken pre-migration, all 17 jobs valid) confirms the data was valid. The `jobs.json.migrated.2` backup (post-race, 1 job remaining) confirms the outcome.

### Expected behavior

The gateway's cron migration changelog states: *"preserving existing isolated cron jobs after Codex harness migration"*. All 17 valid cron jobs should survive any number of config reloads. The split migration should be atomic (either complete, or not started).

### Actual behavior

16 of 17 recurring cron jobs were silently dropped. No error surfaced to the user, no log entry at WARN or above (the "skipped invalid persisted job" message is at debug/info level). The user discovered the loss 5 days later when scheduled work stopped. Only Memory Dreaming Promotion (agentId=null, sessionTarget="isolated") survived.

### OpenClaw version

2026.5.18

### Operating system

Linux 6.1.0-37-amd64 (Debian 12)

### Install method

npm global (via nvm, Node.js v24.2.0)

### Model

N/A (cron infrastructure bug, not model-dependent)

### Provider / routing chain

N/A

### Logs, screenshots, and evidence

**Pre-migration backup** (`~/.openclaw/cron/jobs.json.migrated`, 17 jobs, all valid):
```
$ node -e "const m=await import('./dist/persisted-shape-GNFStPXn.js');
  const jobs=JSON.parse(require('fs').readFileSync('...jobs.json.migrated')).jobs;
  console.log(jobs.filter(j=>m.t(j)===null).length);"  → 17 (all valid)
```

**Post-race backup** (`~/.openclaw/cron/jobs.json.migrated.2`, 1 job):
- Only Memory Dreaming Promotion survived

**Config audit** showing the 9 rapid writes that triggered the race:
```
2026-06-12T05:43:01.923Z | config.write | agents.list[4].tools.allow
2026-06-12T05:43:04.046Z | config.write | agents.list[4].tools.deny
2026-06-12T05:43:06.302Z | config.write | agents.list[4].tools.exec
2026-06-12T05:43:08.517Z | config.write | channels.whatsapp.groupAllowFrom
2026-06-12T06:01:44.074Z | config.write | plugins install
2026-06-12T06:01:58.787Z | config.write | plugins enable
2026-06-12T06:02:02.501Z | config.write | agents.list[4].tools.allow (retry)
2026-06-12T06:02:05.887Z | config.write | agents.list[4].tools.deny (retry)
2026-06-12T06:02:07.884Z | config.write | config unset
```

**Watcher runs** (last run before wipe, never ran again):
```
2026-06-12T09:00:31+0800 | alerts=0 discovered=1424 errors=0  ← last working run
2026-06-13 onward: no runs                                     ← all cron jobs gone
```

**Root cause code paths** (all in `dist/server-cron-CKQu4czX.js`):

1. `ensureLoaded` - the load path where jobs are dropped:
```js
normalized = normalizeCronJobInput(raw);          // can throw
} catch (error) {
    if (!isInvalidCronSessionTargetIdError(error)) throw error;
    normalized = null;                            // job silently killed
}
const hydrated = normalized && typeof normalized === "object" ? normalized : job;
const invalidReason = getInvalidPersistedCronJobReason(hydrated);
if (invalidReason) { /* skipped with info-level log only */ continue; }
```

2. `persist` with stateOnly:true - never clears needsSplitMigration:
```js
updatedCache.needsSplitMigration = stateOnly && migrating;
// When stateOnly=true and migrating=true: needsSplitMigration remains true
```

3. `stop()` is non-blocking - only stops the timer:
```js
// reload handler calls stop() then immediately creates new CronService
// stop() does NOT await ongoing locked operations
```

### Impact and severity

- **Affected:** All cron jobs with sessionTarget:"main" on gateways that trigger the split-migration race. Any gateway with inline-state cron stores is vulnerable on first config reload after startup.
- **Severity:** High — silent data loss of all user-configured cron jobs. No user-visible error or warning.
- **Frequency:** Triggered on first hot reload after startup when needsSplitMigration is active. In our case, 9 rapid config writes made it near-certain.
- **Consequence:** 5 days of silently missed scheduled work across all agents (no deal scans, no nightly summaries, no morning agendas, no Codex usage monitoring, no backup health checks, no calendar reconciliation).

### Additional information

**Last known good:** Pre-migration cron stores (version < 2026.5.18 with inline state) worked correctly because no split migration existed.

**Workaround:** After the lost jobs are restored from `jobs.json.migrated` backup, the split migration is complete and subsequent hot reloads do not trigger the race. The bug only strikes once — on the transition from inline-state to split-state format.

**Proposed fixes (ranked):**
1. Clear `needsSplitMigration` in a single atomic persist — don't split across stateOnly+full writes
2. Make `stop()` await the current `state.op` promise before yielding
3. Elevate the "skipped invalid persisted job" message to WARN level with job ID
4. Add a pre/post job count comparison in `ensureLoaded` that WARNs on unexpected drops
5. Gate `forceReload` to avoid redundant reloads during hot reload cascades

**Reproduction scripts attached at:**
- `/tmp/repro-cron-race.mjs` — standalone Node.js demo of the race pattern
- `/tmp/repro-with-opencclaw.mjs` — uses actual OpenClaw modules to validate backup data

target.title: fix(memory-core): index memory path in FTS text for filename queries (fixes #94102)
target.body:
## Summary

- Fixes #94102.
- `memory_search` FTS rows indexed only chunk body text, so queries for date-like filename stems (e.g. `2026-06-17-1649`) returned unrelated files with `textScore: 0` even when the target file was indexed and content search worked.
- Include the memory file path in the FTS `text` column at index time (`${entry.path}\n${chunk.text}`) so filename/date tokens participate in hybrid keyword ranking.
- Vector embeddings and chunk body text are unchanged.
- Keyword-search snippets and lexical density scoring strip the indexed path prefix back off, so excerpts show the chunk body (not the path line) and stay consistent with vector-search snippets; the FTS index keeps the path purely for matching.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: Exact filename/date-token queries fail to rank the matching memory file first despite a clean index and working content search.
- Real environment tested: Linux (WSL2) source checkout, Node 22, FTS-only memory backend (`provider: none`, vector disabled).
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  1. Built the local checkout (`pnpm build`).
  2. Ran a redacted behavior probe that indexes two memory files and executes `memory_search` for `2026-06-17-1649` through the bundled memory-core runtime.
- Evidence after fix:
  Terminal transcript:
  ```text
  $ node behavior-probe-memory-filename.mjs
  {
    "top": [
      {
        "path": "memory/2026-06-17-1649.md",
        "textScore": 0.000003905989965114915,
        "score": 1
      }
    ]
  }
  ```

  Snippet-cleanliness guard (run via `node`), asserting the keyword hit returns the chunk body and not the `memory/...` path line:

  ```text
  $ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-search.test.ts
  ✓ searchKeyword filename token ranking (issue #94102) > matches date-like filename stems when path is included in FTS indexed text
    expect(results[0].snippet).toBe("Token has been expired or revoked Google OAuth Testing mode")
    expect(results[0].snippet.startsWith("memory/")).toBe(false)
  Tests  23 passed (23)
  ```
- Observed result after fix: Top result path is `memory/2026-06-17-1649.md` with `textScore > 0`; nearby file `memory/2026-06-17-1701.md` is not returned for the filename stem query. The returned snippet for that hit is the chunk body (`Token has been expired or revoked Google OAuth Testing mode`), not the path-prefixed FTS text.
- What was not tested: Live `openclaw memory search` with Gemini embeddings against a production workspace (requires provider credentials and reindex).

## Tests and validation

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-search.test.ts` — 23 passed.
- `pnpm tsgo:test:extensions` — exit 0.
- Regression coverage: filename/date stem ranks first with `textScore > 0`, and the keyword snippet equals the chunk body (asserts it does not start with the `memory/` path prefix).

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? Yes
Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? No
Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? No
Highest-risk area:
Hybrid memory search keyword ranking for existing indexes
Mitigation:
Change applies on reindex/write paths only; no config surface. Existing indexes need reindex to pick up path tokens in FTS text.

## Current review state

Next action:
Await CI + ClawSweeper review
Waiting on:
Maintainer review
Bot comments addressed:
Updated Real behavior proof after initial mock-only-proof gate failure

AI-assisted: yes
